title: NBEP 2026 CCMP
text:
This 2026 Revision to the Comprehensive Conservation and Management Plan (CCMP) for the Narragansett Bay region is a ten-year blueprint for collaborative action among partners of the Narragansett Bay Estuary Program (NBEP) to sustain and restore Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, the Coastal Salt Ponds, and their watersheds in Rhode Island (RI), Massachusetts (MA), and Connecticut (CT), hereafter “Narragansett Bay region.” The estuaries, rivers, streams, and watersheds of the Narragansett Bay region are home to diverse and vibrant habitats, wildlife, and human communities. This CCMP Revision aims to build upon conservation successes while addressing legacy and emerging threats to this beautiful landscape and its inhabitants.
NBEP is a catalyst for scientific inquiry and collective action to investigate, document, restore, and protect water quality, wildlife, and quality of life in the Narragansett Bay region. Since NBEP’s last CCMP Update (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2012), NBEP has intentionally broadened its geographic and organizational focus to emerge as a partnership-based, service-oriented organization rooted in science and dedicated to action throughout the 2,000 square mile Narragansett Bay region. The 113 actions in the 2012 CCMP have been consolidated into 24 priority opportunities for collaborative work. All 24 Actions arose from an intensive five-year period of input from NBEP committee members and other partners. Each Action has been carefully considered through four strategic lenses: regionalism, connectivity, resiliency, and fairness. In highlighting these strategic lenses, NBEP strives to acknowledge and center the Narragansett Bay region’s long, shared history of industrialization and habitat fragmentation that set the stage for current challenges. At the same time, NBEP looks to the strategic lenses for inspiration to rightfully restore a landscape that has provided so much to its residents, and to capitalize on the region’s strengths and opportunities to shape a lasting future for our water, wildlife, and ways of life.

title: Action Water-4.1: Trash and Marine Debris
text: Prevent and remove aquatic trash and marine debris.
crumbs: Action Water-4.1: Trash and Marine Debris

title: Action Water-4.1: Trash and Marine Debris
text: Prevent and remove aquatic trash and marine debris.
crumbs: Action Water-4.1: Trash and Marine Debris

title: Action Water-4.1: Trash and Marine Debris
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action combines efforts to prevent the creation of trash at its source through education and regulation with its removal through technology and coordinated cleanups. Efforts can capitalize on regional plans to create action on preventing and removing trash and debris. Examples include NOAA’s 2024 Southern New England Marine Debris Action Plan and EPA’s Trash Free Waters Program.
Aquatic trash and marine debris harm water quality, endanger plants and animals, and degrade waterbodies that support recreation and tourism. Plastic is particularly problematic due to its ubiquitous presence, longevity, and tendency to break into smaller pieces (Fulfer and Walsh 2023). Trash can also worsen flooding and disrupt wastewater operations by clogging waterways and infrastructure.
Outreach and education can motivate best practices for retailers and consumers to prevent trash from reaching waterways. Policies and legislation can also help reduce trash at its sources. For example, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and many municipalities in Massachusetts have single-use plastic bag bans. Policies to extend end-user responsibilities like “bottle bills” can facilitate recycling through a cash deposit system. Massachusetts and Connecticut operate bottle deposit systems and efforts are ongoing to establish a system in Rhode Island.
Dozens of organizations champion multifaceted efforts to remove trash and marine debris from the environment. For example, Save The Bay is the Rhode Island state coordinator for Ocean Conservancy’s International Coastal Cleanup, which engages partner organizations and volunteers to organize dozens of cleanups and document their findings. In 2024, Rhode Island launched a statewide “Litter-Free Rhode Island” initiative, providing micro-grants to support local cleanup efforts on land and in waterways (Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management, Office of Water Resources 2024). The Commercial Fisheries Research Foundation (CFRF) developed a plan to remove abandoned fishing gear, or “ghost gear,” from coastal Rhode Island waters, including Narragansett Bay; in the first two years of plan implementation, fishermen trained to support the project removed over 10,000 pounds of gear. Beyond cleanup efforts, various interception technologies can work around the clock to remove trash, and there may be opportunities to strategically use them to greater benefit in the Narragansett Bay region.
NBEP will fund priority education, planning, and implementation activities.

title: Action Water-3.1: Centralized Wastewater Infrastructure
text: Prioritize and support wastewater treatment facility and combined sewer system infrastructure upgrades to improve treatment of pollutants and increase resilience.

title: Action Water-3.1: Centralized Wastewater Infrastructure
text: Prioritize and support wastewater treatment facility and combined sewer system infrastructure upgrades to improve treatment of pollutants and increase resilience.

title: Action Water-3.1: Centralized Wastewater Infrastructure
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This action supports continued upgrades at WWTFs and their associated infrastructure to improve treatment of pollutants and increase resilience.
Centralized WWTFs are regulated by delegated state National Pollution Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) programs in Rhode Island and Connecticut, and Environmental Protection Agency National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (EPA NPDES) permits in Massachusetts. Rhode Island and Massachusetts have made strides to improve nutrient treatment at facilities that discharge to Narragansett Bay and its tributaries. Between 2000–2012, summer nitrogen loadings to Narragansett Bay were reduced by 50 percent due to upgrades to nutrient treatment capacity at WWTFs in Rhode Island and Massachusetts. Between 2016–2021, nitrogen loads were reduced by 70–76 percent when compared to pre-nitrogen reduction efforts in the early 2000s (Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management 2024). Further, significant work is ongoing to reduce combined sewer overflows by repairing and replacing sewer infrastructure and constructing large stormwater collection tunnels (see Restored Waters RI).
As of Summer 2025, final design of upgrades to the City of Westerly’s wastewater treatment facility, which discharges to the Pawcatuck River, have been completed to meet new reduced nutrient limits in their Rhode Island Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (RIPDES) permit, with construction planned through fall of 2028. Upgrades may also be necessary at previously upgraded facilities to maintain pollution reductions or add treatment for other pollutants, such as contaminants of emerging concern. Upgrades should be prioritized based on permit compliance status, extreme weather impact risk, and other factors. As of 2018, RIDEM has included a resiliency planning condition in wastewater treatment facility RIPDES permits as they are reissued. These planning conditions require that treatment facilities develop a schedule of short- and long-term actions that will be taken to maintain operation and protect key collection and treatment system assets in the event of intensifying weather.
The Water Utility Resilience Program in Massachusetts supports local drinking water and wastewater utilities in their efforts to improve resilience to extreme weather impacts. The Program develops and maintains statewide critical infrastructure maps and service maps to enhance critical infrastructure resilience planning, asset management planning, and adaptation to extreme weather. The ResilientMass Plan (Massachusetts 2023) guides efforts for wastewater utilities to integrate resilience strategies into their operations, addressing extreme weather, flood potential, and aging infrastructure.
Alongside facility upgrades, continuing efforts are needed to address conveyance infrastructure, which has generally deteriorated over time, leading to increased sanitary sewer overflows. Further, increasing the implementation of low-impact development practices in new and redevelopment is necessary to reduce current and future burdens on treatment infrastructure (Action Water-1.2: Land Use and Watershed Planning).
NBEP will support partners in the implementation of this action. In addition, NBEP can work with partners to track permit compliance at WWTFs to prioritize upgrades.

title: Action Water-2.1: Stormwater Guidance and Education
text: Update and standardize stormwater design manuals, support stormwater workforce training and capacity-building, and encourage public education and involvement to reduce stormwater pollution.
crumbs: Action Water-2.1: Stormwater Guidance and Education

title: Action Water-2.1: Stormwater Guidance and Education
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will support the necessary preconditions to accelerate the use and maintenance of effective designs for stormwater best management practices and support public education and involvement to reduce stormwater pollution. Specifically, partners will update and standardize stormwater BMP design manuals; support training and education for BMP design, construction, and maintenance; and encourage public involvement in reducing stormwater pollution.
Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Connecticut maintain stormwater BMP manuals that provide guidance on stormwater management. Connecticut recently updated its manual (Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection 2024). Older manuals for Massachusetts (Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection 2008) and Rhode Island (Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management and Coastal Resources Management Council 2015) will be updated to incorporate scientific advances, regulatory changes, and newer practices for LID site planning and green infrastructure BMP installation and maintenance.
Workforce education and professional development can encourage and support municipalities to implement and properly maintain stormwater BMPs. The SNEP Network Stormwater Planning Series offers free training and technical assistance to guide communities through the process of developing a conceptual design for a nature-based stormwater retrofit in a selected drainage area. The Providence Stormwater Innovation Center monitors water quality and the effectiveness of stormwater BMPs and teaches and trains others about the successes and failures of BMP design, implementation, maintenance, and lifespan. NBEP funded a project to monitor and evaluate the performance of 25 regional green infrastructure projects and offer critical insights for retrofit or repairs for underperforming projects (Kopp 2023).
NBEP and partners will support work to reduce stormwater pollution through enhanced public education campaigns and new public policies. Regional partners will convene to develop strategies to assist municipalities with pollution reduction program development, implementation, and enforcement, and to design innovative and effective public awareness strategies for adoption by local groups. This action for public pollution mitigation and education also includes support for professional training programs for commercial service providers such as landscapers whose practices have consequences for stormwater quality. Professional training programs, certifications, and registries can also elevate BMPs to industry standard practices.
NBEP will fund stormwater manual updates, trainings, and public education activities. NBEP will also support activities to monitor and evaluate the effectiveness of green infrastructure projects.

title: Action Water-1.1: Water Quality Monitoring
text: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for water quality monitoring programs to continue to provide information needed for management decision-making and filling knowledge gaps.

title: Action Water-1.1: Water Quality Monitoring
text: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for water quality monitoring programs to continue to provide information needed for management decision-making and filling knowledge gaps.

title: Action Water-1.1: Water Quality Monitoring
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will advance collection and use of water quality monitoring data to enhance management decision-making, track progress towards environmental and human health protection and restoration targets, and fill priority knowledge gaps.
Water quality monitoring is required for states to ensure compliance with federal and state regulations, inform management decisions to protect environmental and human health, and track changes to the ecosystem through time. Water quality monitoring includes water column chemistry (e.g., nutrient concentrations, dissolved oxygen concentration, temperature, salinity, pH) and biological responses to pollution (e.g., chlorophyll-a concentrations, bacteria concentrations, or macroinvertebrate abundances).
Monitoring data have been collected by multiple groups since the 1950s, including federal, state, and municipal agencies, non-profits, NGOs, universities, and communities. Many groups receive state funding to provide their data, which are used to build and calibrate models, create decision-support tools, and support restoration and environmental targets. Regionally, these groups work collaboratively to identify gaps in data collection, discuss emergent monitoring technologies, and ensure their data are consistent and useful.
Robust monitoring programs are essential to supporting the Clean Water Act and provide important information for assessing and anticipating the changing vulnerabilities of ecosystems and coastal communities. Development, land use changes, and restoration activities can alter water quality. Monitoring efforts need additional staff capacity and funding to expand to areas that require more attention without compromising data collection in other locations.
NBEP will support statewide monitoring strategy development and collaborate with partners across state boundaries to discuss monitoring activities. Participating on the Rhode Island Environmental Monitoring Collaborative, or other regional initiatives focused on monitoring should be a key activity. Additionally, NBEP may convene topic specific working groups to update methodologies, plan monitoring expansion into new locations, and revise protocols to prioritize efficiency and incorporate new partners.
NBEP will work with partners to develop and update monitoring databases and connect users with data. This could include identifying capacity, funding, and support for existing initiatives, such as the Narragansett Bay Data Portal or Snapshot of the Bay.

title: Action Water-1.1: Water Quality Monitoring
text:
Potential Projects

Facilitate Science Advisory Committee and staff participation on the RI Environmental Monitoring Collaborative to provide multi-state support for cross-boundary monitoring needs including coordinating or standardizing monitoring activities and methods, developing new or expanding current monitoring activities (such as water column chemistry, emerging pollutants, or increased focus on embayments), and ensure that monitoring aligns with management questions and needs.
Develop and/or update water quality monitoring databases.
Support expanded water quality monitoring in newly restored areas, such as the Kickemuit River, to establish a scientific basis for considering recreation and shellfish harvesting.
Facilitate Science Advisory Committee participation in the update to the Rhode Island Statewide Water Monitoring Strategy (2026).
Support innovations in monitoring strategies that bring efficiencies to and build capacity for data collection; encourage community science programs that help fill priority data gaps.
Support innovations for funding and staff capacity to continue data collection, analysis, and reporting.

crumbs: Action Public Spaces-2.1: Environmental Health Alerts

title: Action Public Spaces-2.1: Environmental Health Alerts
text: Develop and publicize education campaigns and public alert and incident reporting systems for environmental health risks.
crumbs: Action Public Spaces-2.1: Environmental Health Alerts

title: Action Public Spaces-2.1: Environmental Health Alerts
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will help protect public health by developing and/or publicizing consistent and education campaigns and notification systems to alert resource users to unsafe environmental conditions. Partners will coordinate efforts to design and implement public outreach strategies to target and alert users at the right places, at the right times, and with accurate messaging.
Water quality improvements in the region’s estuaries and rivers have led to overall safer conditions for recreation and shellfish harvest (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). However, episodic harmful algal blooms, raw and partially treated sewage spills, and legacy contamination like mercury and PCBs still present threats to public health. In 2024, EPA issued updated recommendations for monitoring contaminants of emerging concern in locally caught freshwater fish. Raising public awareness of these environmental health risks is necessary to protect the health and enjoyment of those using the region’s recreational water resources.
Massachusetts and Connecticut, but not Rhode Island, have legal requirements for public notification when untreated sewage flows into State waters. In Rhode Island, the Narragansett Bay Commission operates an online Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO) Alarm Map, as required by their Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (RIPDES) permit. The City of Newport, which has one CSO discharge, does not operate an online overflow notification system. Rhode Island wastewater facilities are required to report sewer system overflows to both RIDEM and EPA, but not to the public. A comprehensive web-based public notification system, mandatory for all types of sewage discharges in Rhode Island, would contribute to public awareness and health protection.
State environmental agencies, some state and local health departments, and environmental organizations coordinate to monitor cyanobacteria blooms and toxins. In recent years, advisories have increased in number and duration (Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management, n.d.). Rhode Island’s Department of Environmental Management and Department of Health issue waterbody-specific advisories, usually via signage, as capacity allows.
Legacy contamination of the region’s freshwaters makes most freshwater fish unsafe for human consumption (Kamman et al. 2005). State Departments of Health provide recommendations for restricting fish consumption, especially for at-risk groups. Both Massachusetts and Rhode Island also issue advisories for specific waterbodies when warranted by available data. Public health agencies often work with medical providers and fishing license agencies to convey this information. However, given language barriers and lack of access to health providers among some user groups, additional effort is needed to expand outreach about unsafe finfish consumption.
The States have notification systems in the event shellfish are unsafe for harvest—RIDEM Office of Water Resources publicizes shellfish closures in near real-time through web postings, recorded message hotline, press releases, and email. Digital notification systems are more effective than signs or flags and could have even greater reach if distributed in multiple languages.
NBEP will help synthesize and communicate the science of environmental health risks associated with recreational use of local waters, coordinate partners to design shared multilingual education materials on environmental health risks, and fund capacity and projects to develop comprehensive public alert systems for environmental health risks.

text: Develop policies and awareness campaigns to clarify and ensure legal access to shorelines and inland waterways.

title: Action Public Spaces-1.2: Public Access Protection
text: Develop policies and awareness campaigns to clarify and ensure legal access to shorelines and inland waterways.
crumbs: Action Public Spaces-1.2: Public Access Protection

title: Action Public Spaces-1.2: Public Access Protection
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will improve public access to shorelines and waterways by clarifying legal rights and policies for public rights-of-way and access points. Public awareness campaigns will help ensure those rights are understood and protected.
Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Massachusetts guarantee access to the coastal shore through the Public Trust Doctrine, which holds all submerged lands and water in the intertidal zone and waterward in trust for public use, including boating, fishing, swimming, etc. In Rhode Island, the public is guaranteed lateral shoreline access up to 10 feet landward of the noticeable high tide debris line, though there are pending legal actions against the 2023 Shoreline Access Law. In Connecticut, lateral access is up to the mean high tide line. In Massachusetts, the public can only use the shoreline below the low-tide mark and intertidal zone if involved in fishing, birding, hunting, and boating.
Public access to rivers, streams, and lakes is not universally protected. Most waterfront property is privately owned, so perpendicular access to the riverbank is more limited. As with coastal shorelines, without a legal right-of-way, public travel across private property to access the water is trespassing. Further, dams without portage create significant barriers to aquatic recreation.
Sometimes when a property transfers to a new owner, public access is blocked (Smith 2023). Property owners adjacent to rights-of-way and public access paths may discourage use by installing illegal no trespassing signs, no parking signs, physical barriers, or guards. In a 2023 Rhode Island CRMC survey, 41 percent of residents surveyed said they use local rights-of-way less often due to lack of parking, or feeling unsafe, unwelcome, or unsure about allowable use (Smith 2024). And increasingly, public access points are being lost to sea level rise and erosion in the wake of more intense storms.
Inequities exist in public access to the shoreline for mobility-impaired individuals without access to comparable amenities. Further, many urban residents must travel farther for legal access to high quality public coastal amenities (Twichell et al. 2022). In areas with limited access opportunities, loss or damage to access points due to storms and sea level rise further exacerbates inequities.
NBEP will fund capacity to clarify or develop policies and voice support for ensuring fair legal access to waterways, especially inland waterways where access issues have received less attention and scrutiny. NBEP will fund capacity to create informational campaigns to better educate residents and visitors alike on access and threats to access.

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Vision
Informed and engaged partners are empowered to implement the CCMP.


Goal
Provide foundational knowledge, capacity, and drivers for CCMP implementation.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Priority Opportunities and Challenges
As a catalyst for scientific inquiry and collective action, NBEP provides foundational knowledge, capacity, and drivers for people to act. NBEP fulfills its roles by convening partners, synthesizing and communicating science, funding capacity and projects, and voicing support across three objectives to advance engagement, information and tracking, and regional cooperation.

Engagement
Implementation of the NBEP CCMP requires informed, engaged, and empowered partners contributing collectively to shared goals. NBEP will build on its track record as a neutral convener and “honest broker” by developing a regional Engagement Plan to prioritize and focus NBEP’s engagement with partners and priority audiences (Action: People-1.1).






NotePublic Engagement Highlight: Blackstone River Watershed Needs Assessment Report










NBEP convened a broad group of Blackstone River Watershed interests between 2019 and 2021 to explore local needs. The resulting Blackstone River Watershed Needs Assessment Report (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2021) describes high-priority needs and 20 recommended actions. The first recommendation in the assessment, creation of the Blackstone Watershed Collaborative and hiring of a manager to oversee it, was accomplished in 2021 with NBEP funding. The Blackstone Watershed Collaborative has to date leveraged over $1M in other funding sources to continue its operation and support of small organizations. This project enabled the Blackstone River to join Narragansett Bay’s other major tributary, the Taunton River, in having an up-to-date plan and coordinating organization to lead its implementation.





Information and Tracking
NBEP consistently tracks and reports on the status and trends of ecological conditions of greatest concern for management and public interest. Monitoring, research, data synthesis, and modeling are necessary to ensure that NBEP’s reporting is based on the best available science.
In 2024, NBEP and partners developed a Research Plan to coordinate and foster research necessary to better understand ecosystem change, improve aquatic habitats, and advance work on environmental indicators. Implementation of the NBEP Research Plan (Action: People-1.2) will increase information and understanding to inform, guide, and evaluate management actions. Advancing and increasing use of integrated ecosystem data synthesis and modeling tools (Action: People-2.2) will better equip scientists and managers to make decisions and better understand the complex interactions between water quality, ecosystems, and socioeconomic indicators.
In 2003, NBEP and partners developed a suite of environmental indicators for Narragansett Bay and its watershed (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2003). In 2017, the list was reviewed and expanded to 24 stressor and condition indicators that underlie ecosystem and socioeconomic health (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). Since then, indicators have been updated with new data and focus areas (Appendix A). There is a continued need to refine and improve metrics of existing indicators and develop new environmental and socioeconomic indicators to address priority management issues that may arise (Action: People-2.3).
NBEP strives to communicate effectively with partners and the broader interested public by developing accurate, understandable, and accessible science communication products for different user groups (Action: People-2.1). Data visualizations and dashboards are effective tools to display information about environmental status and trends. Narrative stories, GIS StoryMaps, and digestible fact sheets also translate complex science for broad readership and encourage further inquiry. Information provided in clear, accessible formats can assist resource managers, non-governmental organizations, decision-makers, and others to better understand stressors affecting ecosystem health and to identify how environmental conditions are changing and impacting communities. These communications are foundational for building public support for action and informing management decisions, including strategic investment in protection and restoration.






NoteInformation and Tracking Highlight: Narragansett Bay Watershed Economy Report










The Narragansett Bay Watershed Economy Report (Uchida et al. 2019) was a collaborative effort that identified 13 key sectors that rely on the watershed’s natural capital and the cumulative economic impact of those sectors ($14B, 2016 dollars).





Regional Cooperation
The Narragansett Bay region requires more funding for conservation, restoration, and resilience projects—and more staff capacity for project development and maintenance—to implement CCMP goals and objectives. Development of the NBEP Finance Plan will ensure NBEP secures sufficient funding to provide its core services and increase funding for CCMP priority projects within the framework of its nine-step Project Development Process (Action: People-3.1).
Once the status and trends of key environmental and socioeconomic indicators are determined, the community must reach consensus on measurable targets for those indicators that equate to a desired future condition (Action: People-3.2).






NoteGovernance Highlight: Massachusetts Division of Ecological Restoration Expansion










The Massachusetts Division of Ecological Restoration (MA DER) was established in 2009 as part of the Massachusetts Department of Fish and Game under the Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs to bring attention, momentum, and capacity to river and wetland restoration. Since then, MA DER and partners have completed more than 160 restoration projects, with over 80 active projects currently underway. Today, MA DER operates three branches (Capacity Building, Habitat Restoration, and Technical Services) and multiple funding programs that support local organizations and project development to improve habitat, reduce flooding, and improve water quality and public safety.





Resiliency Considerations
Higher sea levels, warmer water temperatures, and more intense precipitation and storm surge events will create additional challenges for managing natural, built, and socioeconomic systems in the Narragansett Bay region. Empowering partners and communities with the agency and tools to engage on issues related to regional vulnerability, adaptation, and mitigation are key to expanding resilience. Actions in the People Ready to Act Action Plan develop tools designed to empower partners and communities to take proactive action, advocate for decisions, understand the latest science, and identify management strategies that sustain multiple benefits across various time scales and uncertainties.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
section: People Ready to Act Objectives and Actions
text:
People Ready to Act Objectives and Actions
Providing foundational knowledge, capacity, and drivers for people to act is central to building capacity within the region to protect and restore water and habitat quality, fish and wildlife, and quality of life. The People Ready to Act Action Plan incorporates a suite of interconnected actions to improve regional knowledge, coordination, effectiveness, and capacity to better protect and restore the Narragansett Bay region.







Objectives
Actions




People-1. Increase meaningful and useful partner and public engagement.
Engagement Plan: Develop and Implement the NBEP Engagement Plan to convene and activate partners to implement priority CCMP Actions
Research Plan: Implement the NBEP Research Plan and Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative.


People-2. Improve access to and use of information to improve ecosystem management and public understanding.
Science Communication Tools: Develop compelling open-source data visualizations, stories, and dashboards.
Ecosystem Modeling: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for integrated ecosystem models and decision-support tools that foster effective resource management.
Indicators: Develop and update indicators to track environmental and socioeconomic trends.


People-3. Accelerate CCMP implementation by supporting efforts to increase regional cooperation.
Finance Plan: Develop the NBEP Finance Plan to catalyze fundraising and investments for projects that implement the CCMP.
Regional Targets: Establish shared regional ecosystem targets with public reporting systems to track progress.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: Action People-3.1: Finance Plan
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Develop the NBEP Finance Plan to catalyze fundraising and investments for projects that implement the CCMP.

title: Action People-3.1: Finance Plan
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action calls NBEP to lead development of a Finance Plan to 1) ensure NBEP secures sufficient funding to provide its core services, 2) guide NBEP’s funding decisions on a five to ten-year timescale, and 3) convene efforts to increase funding for priority restoration projects.
In addition to consistent funding through the National Estuary Program, watershed protection efforts in the Narragansett Bay region benefit from an EPA geographic program (the Southeast New England Program (SNEP)) and state funding for conservation, restoration, and resilience projects. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act dollars allocated to NBEP and partners between 2022–2025 have provided essential support for staff capacity, project development, and project implementation.
Since 2022, NBEP’s staff has grown from two to five full-time employees. This growth has enabled NBEP to increase and diversify services that it provides to partners and other organizations, including convening, science communication, and project funding. With the recent hires of two new positions, Watershed Outreach Manager and Staff Ecologist, NBEP anticipates further diversifying its services to include direct outreach and habitat project implementation. The NBEP Finance Plan will seek to balance NBEP’s internal operations requirements with external subawards and contracts to support CCMP implementation.
Due to realized and anticipated reductions in federal investments in conservation efforts over early implementation of this CCMP Revision, it is important for NBEP to develop a targeted strategy for granting subawards and contracts. NBEP will work closely with partners during development of the Finance Plan to identify the highest-priority projects in this CCMP Revision to pursue with external awards within the framework of NBEP’s nine-step Project Development Process (Figure 1).






Figure 1: NBEP Project Development Process



Despite decades of consistent—and in recent years, increased—federal and state financial support for restoration, project funding remains insufficient. The Narragansett Bay region receives roughly one-fifth of the federal dollars per square mile that are received to support comparable project needs in Chesapeake Bay, Puget Sound, Long Island Sound, and Lake Champlain regions. Through development and implementation of its Finance Plan and Engagement Plan (Action: People-1.1), NBEP will convene partners to seek additional funding for water quality and habitat restoration.

title: Action People-2.2: Ecosystem Modeling and Decision-Support Tools
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Support, improve, and create efficiencies for integrated ecosystem models and decision-support tools that foster effective resource management.
crumbs: Action People-2.2: Ecosystem Modeling and Decision-Support Tools

title: Action People-2.2: Ecosystem Modeling and Decision-Support Tools
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will advance the development and use of ecosystem modeling and decision-support tools for filling knowledge gaps, informing key management decisions, and setting and tracking progress toward environmental protection and restoration targets.
Significant environmental research and monitoring has occurred in the Narragansett Bay region since at least the 1950s which has fueled multiple modeling efforts to understand how ecosystems respond to pollution drivers and management actions (Humphries et al. 2022). Applied models focus on answering key management-related questions related to water quality, food-chain dynamics, fisheries resources, and larger ecosystem-based challenges. Localized needs have been met with models and other tools that support TMDL development and permitting, prioritize restoration projects, and highlight gaps in current monitoring activities.
There has long been a desire for linked hydrodynamic, water quality, and ecosystem-based models to help increase understanding of the impact of system stressors and responsive management actions. Using monitoring data in models can assist in forecasting future conditions, selecting responsive actions, tracking their effectiveness, and adapting as needed. Ecosystem models can be used to compare multiple future ecosystem scenarios and identify management strategies that sustain benefits for ecosystem, people, and economies across various time scales and uncertainties.
The rising and interlaced challenges of nutrient loading, stormwater pollution, and socioeconomic concerns necessitate the revision, update, and creation of models and other decision-support tools to better understand ecosystem health, evaluate management strategies, and help set and track progress against management targets. Further, not all models or tools are in forms that are available or useful to end users. Data visualizations and dashboards are simple and elegant ways to accurately and broadly communicate information to a wide audience of partners and other end users. Creating tools using free open-source software ensures that they can be widely shared, modified, and accessible to all.
NBEP will collaborate with partners to convene interests across state boundaries to coordinate modeling and tool development. Activities could include assessing current modeling and tool-development projects to avoid duplication of efforts, discussing gaps in monitoring needed to accurately develop or update models and tools, and gaining consensus on a preferred pathway to developing a bay-wide water quality model. NBEP will participate on partner-led committees to develop or improve models or tools. NBEP can partially or fully fund development, update, or implementation of open-source models or tools to answer key management questions. NBEP will regularly provide guidance and updates to ensure modeling and tool development aligns with CCMP actions.

text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Implement the NBEP Research Plan and Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative.

title: Action People-1.2: Research Plan
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will implement the NBEP Research Plan (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2024) to synthesize existing research and, with the guidance of the Science Advisory Committee, prioritize new topics for NBEP investment and identify key knowledge gaps.
The Research Plan unites the scientific interests of NBEP partners to meet the research needs of the CCMP and inform management decisions. Implementation of the Research Plan will:

Further develop metrics and update indicators of environmental and socioeconomic conditions (Action: People-2.3)
Support, improve, and create efficiencies for ecosystem modeling to answer complex questions and improve ecosystem management (Action: People-2.2)
Create shared ecosystem restoration targets that span boundaries (Action: People-3.2)

The Research Plan responds to NBEP Science Advisory Committee (SAC) priorities, including synthesis of existing data, collection of baseline and new data, analysis and modeling, and information sharing. A new Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaboration, composed of NBEP Science Advisory Committee (SAC) members, researchers, managers, and local experts, will focus on the region’s coastal urban sub-watersheds Hydrologic Unit Code 12 (HUC12). The initial focus is on the Providence-Seekonk Rivers, which continue to exhibit water quality concerns including low dissolved oxygen and elevated pathogens. Over the next ten years, as resources allow, NBEP will expand the collaborations into Fall River and the Westerly-Stonington areas.
NBEP will fund implementation of the Research Plan through requests for proposals, direct awards, and contracts.

Compile and improve information sharing on planned and completed research related to the NBEP region.

title: Habitat Protection and Restoration Action Plan
text:
Vision
Resilient and connected landscapes and waterways support healthy habitats and wildlife and provide people with ecosystem services like clean air and water, flood protection, recreation, and sustainable harvest.


Goal
Restore and protect habitats that support wildlife and people.
crumbs: Habitat Protection and Restoration Action Plan

title: Habitat Protection and Restoration Action Plan
text:
Priority Opportunities and Challenges
The Narragansett Bay region, situated in three of the most densely populated states in the nation, is a mosaic of urban and suburban communities surrounded by forested landscapes and a network of rivers and streams. The American Industrial Revolution, which began at Slater Mill on the Blackstone River in the heart of the region over 200 years ago, left a legacy of fragmented habitats and contaminated sediments that remains a heavy influence on human quality of life. Today, extreme weather events interact with legacy problems and ongoing urbanization to create challenging present and future conditions. Yet people and nature persist, and opportunities abound to advance a collective vision of resilient and connected landscapes, waterways, and people.
Habitat conditions vary greatly across the Narragansett Bay region with estuary and watershed conditions generally improving with distance from urban areas. Historically, ecosystems have been fragmented and fish and wildlife migration corridors disconnected by infrastructure built to accommodate the growing human population and industry of the region. More recent suburban sprawl encroaches and impacts forests and coastal wetlands. Changes in precipitation patterns, warming air and water temperatures, and coastal flooding and storms are also degrading habitats.
When ecosystems lose integrity through fragmentation and degradation, they lose their ability to provide essential functions for protecting wildlife, water quality, human well-being, and the regional economy. Across 13 key economic sectors that rely on the Narragansett Bay watershed’s natural capital, an estimated $14 billion in combined revenue and expenditures was at stake in 2016. Tourism contributed 73 percent of the total (Uchida et al. 2019).

Estuarine Habitats
Since European contact, the region has lost more than half of its salt marshes and seagrasses to pollution, development, and higher sea levels. Roughly 4,300 acres of salt marsh habitat and 875 acres of seagrass habitat remain in the region (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017, 2023). While historic seagrass losses resulted mostly from development and disease, ongoing seagrass losses—and lack of restoration successes—are driven by warmer waters and nutrient pollution that accelerate algal growth and reduce the availability of sunlight necessary for seagrass survival. There is an opportunity to galvanize regional efforts to restore seagrass habitat in the region’s estuaries (Action: Habitats-2.3).
Since the early 1900s, industrialization, increased pollution, and the loss of seagrass caused a shift in shellfish communities from scallops to quahogs, which tend to be found in softer sediments. Today, the quahog is Rhode Island’s official state shellfish, and quahogging is a treasured local pastime. Overfishing of wild oysters, observed as early as colonial times, and pollution linked to early 1900’s industrialization led to declines in natural oyster reefs. With cleaner waters and aquaculture lease management regulations, oyster aquaculture today is a valuable fishery (Rice 2006).
In 2018, the University of Rhode Island completed a third bay-wide survey of benthic habitat quality, advancing a 30-year time series during a time of decreasing nutrient inputs. Results showed improvements in benthic habitats bay-wide, but with less improvement in the bay’s uppermost Providence and Seekonk Rivers, Greenwich Bay system, and Allen Harbor, which are closest to pollution sources (Shumchenia and King 2019). NBEP provided funding for the project and developed a StoryMap that describes this complex work (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2020). In general, there is a need for additional monitoring and modeling of benthic habitat dynamics (such as macroalgae, wild oysters, and infauna) to advance conservation and restoration (Actions: People-2.2 and Habitat-1.1).
Critical salt marsh habitat is under threat throughout the Narragansett Bay region from higher sea levels and altered hydrology from legacy agriculture and development, which drive salt marsh deterioration and fragmentation by accelerating erosion and subsidence and outpacing salt marsh accretion. Salt marsh subsidence (especially at creekbanks) is also linked to increased nutrients as added nitrate decreases belowground root growth that leads to soil destabilization (Deegan et al. 2012). Many marshes show signs of plant die-off, erosion, and other stresses (August 2019). The coastline is now less resilient to storms and floods due to the loss of salt marsh. Without action, most of RI’s remaining coastal wetlands will be displaced or destroyed by the end of this century (Coastal Resources Management Council 2015). The saltmarsh sparrow, which only lives and breeds in salt marshes, is currently under review by the US Fish and Wildlife Service for protection as an endangered species. The need is urgent to identify and preserve salt marsh migration corridors and sustain marshes in place (Action: Habitats-3.2).
Salt marsh restoration practitioners are working to buy time for these vulnerable habitats. For example, Save The Bay has pioneered a technique to restore the hydrology of marshes impacted by human impacts from agricultural uses and mosquito ditching through digging shallow creeks or runnels and maintaining a select number of ditch features to facilitate gradual water drainage of impounded water off the marsh surface (Save The Bay 2021). Impounded water causes vegetation, particularly plants that are adapted to dry conditions at low tide, to become stressed and die, resulting in the marsh surface subsiding. Practitioners also place sediment to elevate degraded marshes to make them more resilient to sea level rise. Both techniques aim to sustain salt marshes and their dependent wildlife species, such as the vulnerable saltmarsh sparrow, in place while conservation of adjacent uplands aims to preserve areas for future inland marsh migration. NBEP supports these and other ongoing salt marsh restoration and monitoring projects with funding and technical assistance through convening the Salt Marsh Restoration, Assessment, and Monitoring Program (RAMP), a cooperation among multiple federal, state, local, non-profit, and academic agencies to restore, assess, and monitor salt marshes throughout Rhode Island.






NoteSuccotash Marsh Sediment Placement and Sentinel Site Monitoring





Succotash Marsh, a 182-acre salt-marsh system located within Potters and Point Judith Ponds in South Kingstown, Rhode Island, provides an array of habitats that support a wide assemblage of birds, including threatened salt marsh sparrows, as well as commercial aquaculture and recreational opportunities. Sea level rise threatens Succotash Marsh by subjecting it to more frequent flooding, and surrounding development prevents the marsh from migrating upland. This project, led by the Narragansett Bay Estuarine Research Reserve, will complete the planning and design phases of a sediment placement project to improve the resilience of Succotash Marsh and establish a monitoring plan that includes pre-restoration monitoring at both the project site and a control site.





Freshwater Habitats
The Narragansett Bay region has over 4,000 miles of rivers and streams and 40,000 acres of lakes and ponds. However, many freshwater habitats are impacted by nutrient enrichment, low dissolved oxygen, or other parameters (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017).
The region’s industrial past has left thousands of dams, impoundments, and other barriers that reduce the connections between freshwater and saltwater systems. There are about 1,000 dams and many times that number of culverts in the Narragansett Bay region. These barriers may not only increase flood risk with increased precipitation, warm waters, and create slow-moving impoundments that harbor invasive aquatic plants, but many also block migration for important diadromous fish populations. By one estimate, every mile of river opened to fish passage can contribute more than $500,000 in economic and social benefits (US Fish and Wildlife Service 2011).






Figure 1: Coldwater streams in the Narragansett Bay region.



Regional partners have worked diligently and successfully to remove barriers to aquatic organism passage on the Narragansett Bay region’s two Wild and Scenic River networks, the Taunton and the Wood-Pawcatuck (see Note 1), as well as on coastal rivers and streams throughout the region. Despite progress, many opportunities remain to capitalize on historic investments to restore and improve freshwater-saltwater connectivity for fish passage, flood resiliency, habitat restoration, and recreation, especially where deteriorating infrastructure can be removed or redesigned for multiple ecological, economic, and social co-benefits. Progress on these complex projects can be accelerated with proactive and collaborative decision-making (Actions: Habitats-2.1 and Habitat-2.2).
For example, while water quality in the Blackstone River has dramatically improved, significant barriers to fish passage remain. The Lower Blackstone Fish Passage Project aims to reinvigorate progress on establishing diadromous fish passage across four dams on the lower Blackstone River in Pawtucket, RI. Fish passage past the four dams would deliver a wide range of important benefits, including opening 206 acres of spawning nursery habitat, generating over 200,000 adult spawning river herring and 10,000 adult spawning shad per year, creating forage fish for commercial species in the bay, improving connectivity for other species, and enabling greater recreational and cultural enjoyment. NBEP led partners to evaluate past efforts, convene workshops, and conduct interviews and mediation among 22 diverse partners to maximize benefits to ecosystems, hydropower, and heritage. In 2022, NBEP transitioned to a funder role and now supports the capacity of lead partners and a newly-formed Community Advisory Committee to continue convening, advocacy, and planning work on the largest fish passage project in the Narragansett Bay watershed, with over $500,000 of Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and Section 320 funds allocated by NBEP to date.






Note 1: Pawcatuck River Fish Passage Projects





Nearly all 34 miles of the Pawcatuck River are open for fish passage for diadromous species such as American shad, alewife, blueback herring, and American eels thanks to several projects constructed over the last fifteen years. Fishways have been completed at Kenyon Mill Dam (Richmond, RI 2013), Upper Shannock/Horseshoe Falls (Richmond, RI 2012), and the Bradford Dam (Bradford, RI 2017). The Lower Shannock Falls Dam (Richmond, RI 2010) and the White Rock Dam (Westerly, RI and Stonington, CT 2015) have been removed.
In 2024, the Town of Westerly and partners won funding from NOAA to implement fish passage or dam removal at the Potter Hill Dam, the last fish passage barrier on the mainstem of the Pawcatuck River. The work will provide access to more than 3,000 acres of spawning habitat and 120 miles of stream habitat for river herring and other migratory fish.
Looking to headwater streams, in 2022, NBEP provided IIJA funds to the Wood-Pawcatuck Watershed Association (WPWA) to develop a cold-water stream restoration plan. WPWA completed the plan in 2024 and now seeks to implement the eight highest-priority projects identified in the plan.





Upland Habitats
The Narragansett Bay region encompasses more than one million acres. About 200,000 acres are protected open space, including natural, agricultural, and recreational lands. More than 260,000 acres in the watershed have been identified as high ecological value, but 185,000 acres of these natural lands remain unprotected as of NBEP’s last regional assessment (Chapter 21 in Narragansett Bay Estuary Program (2017)). Coastal basins are the most urbanized, ranging from 65 to 85 percent urban lands. Impacts to forests are accelerating as suburban sprawl encroaches. For example, between 2001–2011, the Upper- and Middle-Taunton River Basins lost nine percent and 18 percent of their forest lands, respectively, to new development (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). Clear-cutting forests to build ground-mounted solar photovoltaic arrays is a growing concern, but there are opportunities to develop and implement land use policies to advance and balance habitat and floodplain protection as well as clean energy and housing needs Figure 2. Development pressure points to the urgent need to proactively protect remaining lands with high ecological integrity.






Figure 2: Protected open space and solar arrays in the Narragansett Bay region (2024).









Figure 3: Land use (2021) in Narragansett Bay region watersheds.





Resiliency Considerations
Higher sea levels and more intense precipitation and storm surge events will create additional challenges for protecting and restoring natural habitats in the Narragansett Bay region. Restoration and habitat protection plans must continue to prioritize long-term ecosystem adaptation and resilience under changing conditions, rather than simply restoring habitats to some prior state. Understandings of changing environmental conditions must be incorporated into restoration project prioritization, design, implementation, and maintenance. For example, because sea level rise is driving salt marsh deterioration and fragmentation by accelerating and outpacing salt marsh accretion, conservation of upland migration corridors will be necessary for marsh migration.
Warming water temperatures have reduced the number of cold-water river species and have impacted viability of estuarine fish and shellfish communities. Moreover, invasive species are establishing new ranges and outcompeting natives—changing the species compositions and ecosystem services of key estuarine and watershed habitats. Fisheries managers, in particular, will need access to up-to-date data and analyses to be adaptive to new-to-the-region species. Dams, impoundments, culverts, altered streams, and other infrastructure are increasingly vulnerable under altered hydrologic regimes associated with rising sea levels and more intense precipitation and storm surge events that cause flooding. Restoring habitat connectivity for diadromous species provides refuge, increased reproductive opportunity, and resilience to changing water temperature, salinity, and pH.

title: Habitat Protection and Restoration Action Plan
section: Habitat Protection and Restoration Objectives and Actions
text:
Habitat Protection and Restoration Objectives and Actions
Protecting and restoring priority habitats in this Action Plan requires creating and updating restoration plans, implementing restoration projects to improve resilience and connectivity, and protecting existing natural lands through land use policy and acquisition. These efforts require coordinated planning and implementation across multiple geographical scales and political boundaries. Success also requires outreach to local conservation groups and municipalities to support staff capacity and share best practices.







Objectives
Actions


Habitat-1. Improve collection and use of habitat data for decision-making.
Biodiversity and Habitat Quality Monitoring: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for monitoring and documenting biodiversity and habitat quality.


Habitat-2. Improve habitat resilience
Habitat Plans: Update existing and develop new regional habitat restoration plans and strategies to facilitate cross-boundary projects and address emerging challenges for habitat resilience.
Habitat Projects: Identify, prioritize, and coordinate partner opportunities and funding to implement habitat restoration projects.
Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration: Assess and restore benthic habitats to support dynamic estuarine ecosystems.


Habitat-3. Restore habitat connectivity and function
Aquatic Connectivity: Reconnect freshwater and saltwater systems and improve connectivity within freshwater systems to restore habitats and fisheries.
Salt Marsh Restoration: Restore coastal wetlands and reconnect with uplands for habitat migration, wildlife, and recreation.

title: Action Habitat-3.1: Aquatic Connectivity
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Reconnect freshwater and saltwater systems and improve connectivity within freshwater systems to restore habitats and fisheries.

title: Action Habitat-3.1: Aquatic Connectivity
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will accelerate planning and implementation of projects that improve and restore connectivity within freshwater systems and between freshwater and saltwater systems, such as removing dams and impoundments, replacing culverts, creating fish passageways, naturalizing river channels, and restoring tidal connections. These projects will improve safety, instream water quality and hydrology, wildlife habitat, and cultural and recreational opportunities. To deliver ecosystem benefits, structural improvements should be accompanied by system or sub-watershed scale improvements to reduce stormwater, nutrients, and contaminants (CCMP Water Quality Improvement Action Plan) (Bernhardt and Palmer 2011; Wohl et al. 2015).
Many dams and undersized culverts were not built to accommodate more intense storms and nuisance flooding, therefore posing significant threats if they fail (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2021). Furthermore, abundances of diadromous forage fishes for commercially important fisheries like herring, shad, and eel have declined due to being cut off from spawning habitats. The extent of local cold-water fisheries like brook trout in watershed headwaters are limited by lack of adequate localized habitat connectivity. Resident fish, invertebrates and other aquatic life are also impacted negatively.
Efforts to remove barriers to address safety concerns, improve fishery habitat, and address flood risk from undersized infrastructure have increased significantly over the last decade. For example, from 2010 to 2017, seven dams were removed from the Pawcatuck River and replaced with nature-like rock fishways to allow fish to move 20 miles upstream to historical spawning areas. Remaining restoration priorities include extending passage on the Pawcatuck River with a focus on cold-water streams in the upper watershed, removing barriers in tributaries of the Taunton River, and establishing fish passage between the Blackstone River and Narragansett Bay for the first time since the Industrial Revolution. In 2019, partners convened to reignite planning for the Lower Blackstone Fish Passage Project to achieve fish passage over the four lowest dams on the river.
Despite progress, many challenges face restoration practitioners. Chief among these is the cost of implementing a project from start to finish—often over $1 million to achieve design, permitting, and construction on a single road culvert. With thousands of undersized culverts and remnant dams across the watershed, there is a clear need to realize efficiencies in design and permitting steps to make any meaningful progress toward connectivity goals. Also important is the presence of contaminated sediments in the region’s river systems, particularly those that have seen industrial use like the Blackstone River. The possible threat of contaminated sediments often stops dam removal projects before they even begin, yet current information about sediment quality in the region’s rivers is nonexistent or difficult to find. A comprehensive effort to document information about sediment quality and fill information gaps where needed would accelerate restoration efforts.
NBEP will convene workgroups to share information, identify and prioritize projects, realize efficiencies to accelerate projects, support work to better understand sediment quality in the region’s rivers, and maintain priority project databases. NBEP will also help fund community outreach and project planning for priority dam removal, culvert replacement, and fish passage projects.

title: Action Habitat-3.1: Aquatic Connectivity

Identify and prioritize opportunities to restore diadromous fish and to address high-hazard barriers; develop site-specific information and tools to assist decision-making, including cost-benefit analyses.
Conduct a comprehensive assessment of sediment contamination in the Blackstone River and its tributaries, as well as other rivers and streams with widespread sediment contamination concerns, to inform dam removal efforts.
Partner with federal, state, and local agencies, officials, and indigenous communities to secure funding for large connectivity construction projects, including Lower Blackstone and other high priority areas.
Conduct training opportunities to assess culverts and dams for safety, resilience to heavy precipitation events, and aquatic connectivity restoration opportunities.
Incorporate North Atlantic Aquatic Connectivity Collaborative (NAACC) connectivity data into decision-making processes for culvert replacements.
Engage communities in connectivity efforts to understand community priorities, provide information about common concerns like toxic sediments, water quality, water level changes, and organize support for connectivity projects.
Work with Dam Safety offices to incorporate potential alternatives into dam emergency scenarios that account for connectivity restoration opportunities.

title: Action Habitat-2.2: Habitat Projects
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This action will enhance regional coordination and implementation of habitat restoration projects. Priority habitats for restoration include salt marshes, seagrass beds, tidal shorelines, rivers, riparian shorelines, floodplains, wetlands, ponds, and lakes. Restoration projects are designed to improve water quality, natural water flow and sediment regimes, and health of native freshwater and marine life. Habitat restoration also supports resilience by increasing flood protection from storms and higher sea levels. Restored and resilient habitats improve community access and health and economic prospects for commercially important harvests.
Habitat restoration in the region has generally been opportunistic, with practitioners implementing projects as they arise when willing landowners, staff capacity, and funding align. Salt marsh practitioners participating in the Salt Marsh RAMP have made significant progress on collaboratively prioritizing restorations based on condition, value, and migration potential; this coordination can serve as a model for other habitat types. NBEP will continue to support or fund smaller projects, but this action will accelerate coordinated and holistic efforts directed at system or sub-watershed scale improvements. Recovery of ecological systems and natural processes in a degraded watershed requires restoration at the appropriate scale (Wohl et al. 2015).
NBEP will maintain priority restoration project databases and coordinate funding opportunities for priority projects through existing federal sources like EPA and NOAA, and state sources like the RI Coastal and Estuarine Habitat Restoration Program and Trust and the MA Coastal Resilience Grant Program.
NBEP will also coordinate outreach and project support to local watershed or lake and pond associations to help implement restoration projects. These groups are local experts in providing outreach and education to their communities and many are ready to partner and collaborate on habitat restoration projects.

title: Action Habitat-1.1: Biodiversity and Habitat Quality Monitoring
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will monitor and document the region’s biodiversity, habitat quality, and habitat restoration project impacts.
Significant monitoring of the Narragansett Bay region’s natural habitats and biodiversity takes place at broad and project-specific scales. Biodiversity and habitat monitoring is integral to understanding the impacts of upland development, restoration efforts, changing water quality, impacts of extreme weather, and socioeconomic pressure on natural resources and biodiversity.
Monitoring has been managed by multiple groups—federal, state, and municipal agencies, non-profits, NGOs, universities, and communities—that collaborate to establish protocols to ensure consistent and useful data are delivered to resource managers, decision-makers, and the public. While many agencies and organizations freely share their data, locating and accessing these data can be challenging. In addition, maintaining funding and capacity (both paid and volunteer) to collect, quality control, evaluate, and publish monitoring data remains a perennial challenge. Integrating existing monitoring data strategically can improve efficiency, highlight spatial or temporal gaps, and inform capacity issues.
Post-restoration monitoring is essential to determine restoration successes and lessons learned, as well as determining the long-term ecosystem resilience capabilities of restored habitats. Challenges such as extreme weather, rising seas, and rising temperatures will impact habitats and restoration project outcomes. Monitoring these changes is important for assessing and anticipating the changing management and restoration needs of ecosystems and coastal communities.
NBEP will convene and collaborate with appropriate partners throughout the region to coordinate or otherwise support habitat and biodiversity monitoring activities to address management questions and needs. Activities could include updating methodologies, expanding monitoring into new locations, and revising protocols to prioritize efficiency and incorporate new partners. NBEP will also work with partners to develop and/or update monitoring databases and connect users with habitat and biodiversity data.

ACTION PLAN: Water Quality Improvement
VISION: Waters support healthy ecosystems and communities by meeting their designated uses, including healthy fish and wildlife habitat, drinking water supply, fishing, shellfish harvesting, and recreation.
GOAL: Improve water quality to support healthy ecosystems and communities.

(Left side of 2-page spread)









ACTION STATEMENT
POTENTIAL PROJECTS
OUTPUTS
OUTCOMES




OBJECTIVE 1: Improve collection and use of water quality data for decision-making.





Water 1.1 Water Quality Monitoring: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for water quality monitoring programs to continue to provide information needed for management decision-making and filling knowledge gaps.

Facilitate Science Advisory Committee and staff participation on the RI Environmental Monitoring Collaborative to provide multi-state support for cross-boundary monitoring needs including coordinating or standardizing monitoring activities and methods, developing new or expanding current monitoring activities (such as water column chemistry, emerging pollutants, or increased focus on embayments), and ensure that monitoring aligns with management questions and needs.

Develop and/or update water quality monitoring databases.

Support expanded water quality monitoring in newly restored areas, such as the Kickemuit River, to establish a scientific basis for considering recreation and shellfish harvesting.

Facilitate Science Advisory Committee participation in the update to the Rhode Island Statewide Water Monitoring Strategy (2026).

Support innovations in monitoring strategies that bring efficiencies to and build capacity for data collection; encourage community science programs that help fill priority data gaps.

Water 1.2 Land Use and Watershed Planning: Work at multiple scales to assess land use changes and develop plans and tools to protect watersheds.

OBJECTIVE 2: Reduce pollution from stormwater runoff.





Water 2.1 Stormwater Guidance and Education: Update and standardize stormwater design manuals, support stormwater workforce training and capacity-building, and encourage public education and involvement to reduce stormwater pollution.

Update stormwater design and installation manuals for each state, as necessary, including protocols for maintenance, monitoring, and evaluation. NBEP has partnered with RIDEM to secure funding to update the Rhode Island Stormwater Design Manual.
