section: ACTION PLAN: Habitat Protection and Restoration
text:
ACTION PLAN: Habitat Protection and Restoration
VISION: Resilient and connected landscapes and waterways support healthy habitats and wildlife and provide people with ecosystem services like clean air and water, flood protection, recreation, and sustainable harvest.
GOAL: Restore and protect habitats to support wildlife and people.

(Left side of 2-page spread)









ACTION STATEMENT
POTENTIAL PROJECTS
OUTPUTS
OUTCOMES




OBJECTIVE 1: Improve collection and use of habitat data for decision-making.





Habitat 1.1 Biodiversity and Habitat Quality Monitoring: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for monitoring and documenting biodiversity and habitat quality.

Convene interstate interests to coordinate and standardize monitoring activities and methods, including developing new or expanding current monitoring activities (such as seagrass, salt marsh, macroalgae, and cyanobacteria monitoring), and ensure that monitoring aligns with management questions and needs.

Develop and/or update natural heritage databases for the states as needed, including BORIIS2 and BioMap2.

Support development of partner-driven habitat and biodiversity monitoring data clearinghouse tools.

Develop data visualization tools to encourage coordination on restoration projects.

Support innovations in monitoring strategies that bring efficiencies to and build capacity for data collection and encourage community science programs that help fill priority data gaps.

Support partners to develop and expand sentinel habitat monitoring sites to explore changes to the habitat with time and under changing conditions

Convene and support partners to explore seagrass restoration using seed-dispersal and other techniques.


New monitoring protocols or programs from new or expanded monitoring activities

Updated natural heritage databases and State Wildlife Action Plans

Clearinghouse tool connecting users with data

Data visualization tools highlighting restoration and monitoring efforts

Working group meetings to convene on priority topics in the watershed

Reports on select monitoring objectives


Coordinated and efficient monitoring activities

Improved collection and use of natural heritage information

Enhanced understanding of ecosystem monitoring gaps and mechanisms to fill them

Increased understanding of characteristics and changes in the Narragansett Bay region over time

Ecosystem management that is better informed by monitoring



OBJECTIVE 2: Improve habitat resilience.





Habitat 2.1 Habitat Plans: Update existing and develop new regional habitat restoration plans and strategies to facilitate cross-boundary projects and address emerging challenges for habitat resilience.

Update RI Anadromous Fish Restoration Plan.

Update existing restoration strategies to include updated data, information, and completed projects.

Develop a Narragansett Bay region salt marsh restoration strategy or update the RI Coastal Wetland Restoration Strategy to include the upper part of Narragansett Bay in Massachusetts and portions of Little Narragansett Bay in Connecticut, where useful. Strategy should highlight areas where marsh migration is possible and should be supported.

Support the development and implementation of the Rhode Island Shellfish Restoration and Enhancement Plan.

Develop a Narragansett Bay and/or broader regional submerged aquatic vegetation restoration plan that reviews restoration strategies that have been tried in Narragansett Bay to date, including current seed-based restoration strategies, and assesses whether recent water quality improvements could improve restoration success. This restoration plan should also include new data being collected on flowering phenology and seed collection and seed transfer guidance.

Expand and update Sea Level Affecting Marsh Migration (SLAMM) models to include the Palmer and Runnins Rivers and other areas of MA, CT, and RI that were not covered in state models.

Pursue or expand state level management plans for lakes and ponds, inclusive of invasive species and educational outreach to boaters.

Develop plans and actions to protect cold-water streams and rivers, or specific endangered and/or threatened freshwater species, such as native brook trout.


RI Shellfish Restoration and Enhancement Plan

A multi-state salt marsh restoration strategy

At least one regional submerged aquatic vegetation restoration strategy

Updated freshwater restoration and conservation plans and protocols

Coordinated meetings among habitat restoration practitioners

Expanded SLAMM to cover entire region seamlessly


Increased coordination on habitat restoration priorities, techniques, and projects across boundaries

Shared habitat restoration goals, objectives, and targets

Better designed and more impactful estuarine restoration projects

Healthier and more sustainable estuarine habitat

Increased coordination on freshwater restoration priorities, techniques, and projects across institutional and state boundaries

Shared plans for achieving freshwater restoration goals, objectives, and targets



Habitat 2.2 Habitat Projects: Identify, prioritize, and coordinate partner opportunities and funding to implement habitat restoration projects.

Coordinate among groups pursuing priority habitat assessment, planning, and restoration in the greater Narragansett Bay region.

Maintain priority project lists for major estuarine, riverine, and riparian habitat types/ecosystems in the Narragansett Bay region.

Support states, municipalities, and local NGOs with planning, project identification, and prioritization.


Coordination meetings and tools for groups pursuing restorations in the same areas

Annually updated list of priority estuarine and freshwater restoration projects

Additional fully funded requests for proposals for freshwater and estuarine habitat projects

Additional completed restoration projects from approved restoration plans.

Education and invasive species removal events and programs


Accelerated system or sub-watershed scale habitat improvements

Activated and coordinated local partners in restoration and outreach projects

More engaged watershed or pond groups with more members

More estuarine and freshwater systems meet both habitat and recreational use standards and sustain healthy wildlife and human use



Habitat 2.3 Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration: Assess and restore benthic habitats to support dynamic estuarine ecosystems.

Synthesize past and ongoing regional seagrass restoration efforts including methodologies used, success of restoration efforts, locations, etc. This synthesis should assess if these techniques could be more successful with current, improved water quality.  | - White papers synthesizing seagrass and artificial reef restoration efforts  | - Activated and coordinated local partners in assessment and restoration projects

Update seagrass site suitability models or indices (Detenbeck and Rego 2015). Ensure such models include environmental variables of interest including water quality, sediment type and quality. Focus should be given to shallow water habitats to ensure data coverage is of sufficient resolution for a model.  | - Updated Seagrass Site Suitability models  | - Increased number of benthic habitat restoration projects  |
Update monitoring protocols to modernize approaches to tiered seagrass monitoring, include widgeon grass, and develop sentinel seagrass monitoring sites. Sentinel sites should focus on how habitats respond to stress, establishing baselines for restoration, and include an area currently covered with widgeon grass to assess any potential transition from widgeon grass to eelgrass.  | - Updated seagrass, macroalgae, and benthic habitat monitoring protocols  | - More acres of benthic sediment habitat restored

Establish long-term goals for benthic habitat monitoring, including the use of sediment profile imaging, sled-cameras, gliders, and autonomous underwater vehicles.  | - Updated and expanded SPI analysis | - Increased number of artificial reefs providing diverse habitat, and recreation and economic benefits

Update 2018 benthic sediment profile imaging (SPI) for Narragansett Bay. Expand SPI to other areas of the region (Salt Ponds and Little Narragansett Bay) and new sites of interest, including shallow water portions of the coastal ponds. SPI can provide an assessment of benthic habitat quality trends, particularly in the upper part of the Bay as a result of improved water quality.  | | - More acres of seagrass restored
Update benthic sled camera monitoring in the Providence River Estuary.  | |
Synthesize artificial reef work that has been completed throughout the bay assessing the method and materials used, and if the reef has improved fish habitat in the area. Use this synthesis to develop an artificial reef plan to guide future work to focus on both habitat creation and resilience in high-erosion areas.  | |
Implement the Macroalgae Monitoring Protocol developed by SEAC-RI to understand the seasonal abundance/distribution of drift and attached algae, habitat impacts from drift algae, and changes to algae due to stress.  | |
Explore using dredged materials to restore areas of exposed sediment containing high concentrations of contaminants. | |





OBJECTIVE 3: Restore habitat connectivity and function.





Habitat 3.1 Aquatic Connectivity: Reconnect freshwater and saltwater systems and improve connectivity within freshwater systems to restore habitats and fisheries.

Identify and prioritize opportunities to restore diadromous fish and to address high-hazard barriers; develop site-specific information and tools to assist decision-making, including cost-benefit analyses.

Conduct a comprehensive assessment of sediment contamination in the Blackstone River and its tributaries, as well as other rivers and streams with widespread sediment contamination concerns, to inform dam removal efforts.

Partner with federal, state, and local agencies, officials, and indigenous communities to secure funding for large connectivity construction projects, including Lower Blackstone and other high priority areas.

Conduct training opportunities to assess culverts and dams for safety, resilience to heavy precipitation events, and aquatic connectivity restoration opportunities.

Incorporate North Atlantic Aquatic Connectivity Collaborative (NAACC) connectivity data into decision-making processes for culvert replacements.

Engage communities in connectivity efforts to understand community priorities, provide information about common concerns like toxic sediments, water quality, water level changes, and organize support for connectivity projects.

Work with Dam Safety offices to incorporate potential alternatives into dam emergency scenarios that account for connectivity restoration opportunities.


Working group meetings

Updated Rhode Island Anadromous Fish Restoration Plan

A prioritized list of connectivity needs for the six major basins in the region

Additional connectivity project designs completed

Outreach materials to support community engagement about ecosystem changes and post-dam removal, including water level changes, toxic sediments, and water quality


Reconnection of miles of river and acres of habitat

Return of diadromous fish to river systems



Habitat 3.2 Salt Marsh Restoration: Restore coastal wetlands and reconnect with uplands for habitat migration, wildlife, and recreation.

Sustain marshes in place with a suite of hydrology restoration techniques to address legacy human impacts.

Accelerate work to elevate priority subsiding marshes using sediment placement techniques.

Explore beneficial use of dredged material to help marshes keep up with rising seas or to restore areas of exposed sediment with high concentrations of contaminants.

Facilitate marsh migration through land conservation and managed retreat through land acquisition, redevelopment planning, rolling easements, setbacks, and buffer definitions.

Deliver ongoing one-on-one technical assistance to municipal parks and land use programs.

Identify tidal restriction restoration opportunities in concert with other restoration techniques.

Maintain the Salt Marsh RAMP workgroup project tool, MarshMap, and expand the tool to include Massachusetts and Connecticut projects.

title: About NBEP
text:
NBEP is a catalyst for scientific inquiry and collective action to restore and protect water quality, wildlife, and quality of life in the Narragansett Bay region, including Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, the Coastal Salt Ponds, and their watersheds. NBEP envisions clean water and habitat to sustain all who live, work and play in the Narragansett Bay region. Hosted by Roger Williams University (RWU), with a staff of five and governed by a 28-member Steering Committee, NBEP is the only partner-led organization pursuing place-based conservation across the three-state Narragansett Bay region of Rhode Island, Massachusetts, and Connecticut.
NBEP was one of the first four estuaries to be included in the National Estuary Program (NEP), which is now 28 members strong. The NEP, administered by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), was created by Congress through 1987 Amendments to the federal Clean Water Act. The enabling legislation was drafted by late Rhode Island Senator John H. Chafee and other leaders in Congress to protect and restore “estuaries of national significance.” The NEP designation provided funding and structure for the nascent Narragansett Bay Program to publish the results and recommendations from seven years of intensive study of Narragansett Bay and its watershed as the first Comprehensive Conservation and Management Plan (CCMP) for Narragansett Bay (1992). The 1992 CCMP laid the groundwork for NBEP to operate as a locally-led, collaborative, non-regulatory program focused on protecting and restoring the water quality and ecological integrity of Narragansett Bay. NBEP is one of six NEPs with watersheds in New England. While NBEP engages with all NEPs, NBEP coordinates particularly closely with neighboring Buzzards Bay National Estuary Program and Long Island Sound Partnership due to small study area overlaps (2.8 square miles and 75.6 square miles, respectively). Staff carefully communicate with each other about funded projects and other work within the overlap areas to ensure appropriate levels of support across all communities within NEP regions.

title: About NBEP
text:
Management Conference
NBEP’s shared governance structure, consisting of dedicated staff and interns along with three volunteer Standing Committees, ensures that open discussion, cooperation, consensus-building, and collaborative decision-making among diverse partners advances the CCMP and mission.
Three Standing Committees—Steering Committee, Executive Committee, and Science Advisory Committee—include partners that represent the States in the region, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and other interested Federal agencies, local governments, interstate or regional entities, nonprofit conservation organizations, educational institutions, general public, and the host institution (Table 1). As NBEP’s host institution, Roger Williams University administers program funding and provides fiscal and program oversight. Each committee aspires to include a broad representation of interests that reflect the geographic, economic, and cultural diversity of the region.

The Steering Committee is NBEP’s primary governing body and assists the EPA, Host Institution, and NBEP Executive Director with setting the direction for NBEP. The Steering Committee of 28 members serves as a forum for open discussion, consensus building, and collaborative partner decision-making to advance implementation of the NBEP CCMP and mission.
The Executive Committee is a designated subset of eight members of the Steering Committee that offers counsel on major decisions facing the NBEP Executive Director, Steering Committee, or program.
The Science Advisory Committee provides a forum for NBEP and its partners to examine, discuss, synthesize, and share scientific findings to facilitate integration of science with management decisions, and offers guidance to ensure the scientific integrity of NBEP’s work.

title: About NBEP
text:
NBEP’s Evolution
Over the past 10 years, NBEP intentionally built upon its identity as a funder of research and restoration to emerge as a more collaborative, interdisciplinary, and nimble body that provides a range of services and financial support to projects that respond to local needs and scientific findings across the Narragansett Bay region. To make this transition, NBEP leveraged partnerships to fully acknowledge issues and opportunities throughout the entire watershed and associated estuaries with a key focus on engagement with partners in Massachusetts. NBEP conducted deep relationship-building through hundreds of one-on-one meetings with new partners to expand participation in decision-making and understand how NBEP’s services could fill needs. Since 2018, NBEP’s Steering Committee has added new representation from fishing, health, transportation, non-governmental organizations, and the general public while retaining a nearly balanced membership across Rhode Island and Massachusetts. NBEP’s committee meetings, working groups, and funded projects function as collaboration hubs for these partners to work on common issues and opportunities outside of regular geopolitical, organizational, and cultural boundaries. As of the publication of this 2026 CCMP Revision, NBEP has solidified an organizational niche as a collaborative and partnership-based organization with a service-oriented and science-based approach to addressing complex environmental issues.

title: About NBEP
text:
NBEP’s Services
Guided by science and the shared will of the partnership, NBEP pursues lasting solutions to the region’s interrelated environmental challenges of urbanization, pollution, and a shifting baseline of environmental conditions to improve wildlife habitat and water quality. NBEP catalyzes collective action across geopolitical boundaries by providing collaborative planning services, science synthesis and communication tools, and project funding (Figure 1).






Figure 1: NBEP Services



NBEP’s Guiding Principles

Be bold and maximize sustainable benefits
Integrate and communicate best-available science for management decisions
Build regional collaboration as a neutral convener of varying perspectives
Secure and leverage the unique geopolitical identity of the Narragansett Bay region
Support locally-driven capacity and priorities
Focus on storytelling with people and places that demonstrate the importance of conservation
Be of service to partners


Collaborative Planning
NBEP pursues independent convening, planning, and consensus-building among diverse groups of partners and communities to address shared issues and opportunities. Key examples of NBEP’s collaborative planning work include:

The Blackstone Needs Assessment project, in which NBEP convened Blackstone River Watershed interests to explore local needs, develop the Blackstone River Watershed Needs Assessment Report (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2021a), and establish a local coordinating organization to lead implementation of the report. As of this document’s publication, the Blackstone Watershed Collaborative is an independent nonprofit organization operating as an impact network to promote learning and coordinate action to solve complex watershed issues that no one organization could solve on its own. This work also provided a replicable model for creating the pre-conditions necessary to advance paired ecosystem and community health in other large and complex watersheds in the Narragansett Bay region.
The Lower Blackstone Fish Passage Project, in which NBEP led partners to reinvigorate progress on efforts to establish diadromous fish passage across four dams on the lower Blackstone River in Pawtucket, Rhode Island. In 2022, NBEP transitioned to a funder role and now supports the capacity of lead partners to continue convening, advocacy, and planning work on the largest fish passage project in the Narragansett Bay watershed with over $500,000 of NBEP’s Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds allocated to date.
Science Working Groups that convene partners to investigate and respond to ongoing and emerging topics, including:

Salt Marsh Restoration, Assessment, and Monitoring Program (RAMP): Established in 2020 to formalize a decades-long cooperation between multiple federal, state, local, non-profit, and academic agencies to restore, assess, and monitor salt marshes throughout Rhode Island.
Social Science Working Group: Established in 2020 to advance the integration of current social science into environmental decision-making in the Narragansett Bay region.
Fishermen’s Ecological Knowledge Working Group: Established in 2021 to create a collaborative multidisciplinary team to assess how best to integrate fishermen’s observations into existing ecological monitoring frameworks.
Seaweed Ecology and Assessment Collaborative (SEAC-RI): Established in 2024 to understand the role macroalgae plays in the ecosystem and how it impacts seagrasses, oyster beds, and other habitats throughout the region.
Submerged Aquatic Vegetation Working Group: Established in 2020 and revitalized in 2025 to advance seagrass monitoring and restoration in Rhode Island waters.
Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative: Established in 2024 to provide cross-state collaboration and coordination to understand ecosystem change in urban coastal waters informing resource management, resilience, and habitat improvement.




Science Synthesis and Communication
NBEP provides scientific data analysis and communication using innovative storytelling and open-source web application tools to investigate complex socioenvironmental topics. NBEP serves as a hub for information-sharing tools such as:

2017 State of Narragansett Bay and its Watershed Report: This technical report, co-authored by NBEP and partners, describes 24 environmental and socioeconomic condition and stressor indicators that assess Narragansett Bay and its watershed (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). Since publication, NBEP has updated numerous indicators with more recent data, added new focus areas to existing indicators, and expanded indicators to cover the entire Narragansett Bay region to include the Rhode Island Coastal Ponds, Little Narragansett Bay, and their respective watersheds (See Appendix A).
A publicly-accessible GIS Data Hub that facilitates data-sharing and hosts interactive maps and StoryMaps to dive into complex topics, including:

How Do We Use Our Coasts? StoryMap (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2021b): NBEP, alongside partners at US EPA, used anonymized cell phone visitation data to assess usage of public coastal access sites.
Hundred Acre Cove Septic Viewer and Stormwater Infrastructure Dashboard (Save The Bay 2021): NBEP, in partnership with Save The Bay, created GIS tools to inform actions to better protect water quality in Hundred Acre Cove in the tidal Barrington River.
Clean Water and Quahogs StoryMap (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2022): NBEP created an interactive tool to explain and celebrate the conditional opening of 1,900 acres of the lower Providence River for shellfishing for the first time in 75 years.
Into the Woods: Solar in the Forest StoryMap (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2025): NBEP created a tool to visualize the number and location of ground-mounted solar projects in the Narragansett Bay region and assess how much undeveloped land has been lost to solar development across the region and at subwatershed scales.

Open-source data visualization tools:

WQDashboard (2026): NBEP built a web tool using the Shiny application for R to enable local watershed groups to display water quality data on their websites. As of 2026, the tool has been adopted by the Blackstone River Coalition and the Salt Ponds Coalition.
MarshMap (2025): At the request of the Salt Marsh RAMP Working Group, NBEP built a tool that displays information about salt marsh restoration and monitoring projects in Rhode Island.




Project Funding
NBEP provides watershed project funding that helps build a pipeline of future projects that restore and sustain the natural environment. Funding supports research, staff capacity, and project planning steps, including studies, assessments, and engineering design. NBEP’s far-reaching outreach to partners between 2019–2021 identified a lack of local capacity in state agencies, municipalities, and non-governmental organizations as the single biggest barrier to new project development in the Narragansett Bay region. In response, NBEP developed a nine-step Project Development Process (Figure 2) that aims to communicate the importance of often-underfunded pre-project development steps, from engagement to design and planning. As a result of this outreach and the significant influx of Infrastructure and Jobs Act funds beginning in 2022, NBEP cemented its niche as a funder of pre-construction engagement and planning. While the sunset of Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds will impact NBEP’s ability to support project development, this CCMP Revision lays the foundation for work to secure alternative sustainable funding sources.






Figure 2: NBEP Project Development Process



Since 2019, NBEP has granted over $5 million to priority restoration, research, and community engagement projects (Figure 3), including:

Green Infrastructure Grants (2021, 2022): NBEP awarded $953,988 in §320 funds for stormwater planning projects that align natural and engineering processes to improve water quality and reduce flooding while offering a range of co-benefits, including resilience to increasing rainfall intensity, wildlife habitat, public health, and other socioeconomic and community enhancements.
Capacity-Building Grants (2022, 2023): NBEP awarded over $1 million in §320 and Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds to support staff capacity within seven partner organizations. To date, recipients have more than doubled the amount awarded to them by NBEP in additional grant leverage. The early results of this grant program demonstrate the power of supporting people and organizations that already have relationships and agency to push projects forward.
Habitat Restoration and Public Access Grants (2023): NBEP awarded $748,822 in Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds for seven planning projects to improve watershed habitats and public access to those habitats. Project benefits include improving marsh resilience and creating more waterfront public recreational access points while restoring coastal habitat and improving flood resilience. Grants help fund the critical early pre-construction phases of project planning and design.
Public Outreach and Education Grants (2021–2025): NBEP awarded $167,086 in §320 funds for eleven outreach and education projects seeking to meaningfully involve watershed residents in restoration. From school programs, to work-force development, to volunteer programs and boater outreach, these grants help partners directly educate and engage the public on key watershed issues.
Aquatic Habitat Connectivity Grants (2022, 2024, 2025): NBEP awarded over $2.25 million in Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds for capacity-building and planning projects that seek to remove barriers to aquatic organism passage, including dam removals, culvert upgrades, and fish passage structures. These projects help support important fisheries and better protect communities from flooding.

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text: This 2026 CCMP Revision is a regional call to action for shared efforts that can measurably improve the region’s water quality, wildlife, and quality of life over the next decade. Rather than a comprehensive list of the many ongoing initiatives that will benefit the region’s estuaries and watersheds, this 2026 CCMP Revision contains a targeted set of priority Actions for collaborative work among NBEP staff, organizations represented on NBEP’s committees, and other partners throughout the watershed. The list of priority Actions was crafted using a cooperative process that reflects best available science.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
section: Partner Engagement for Revising the CCMP
text:
Partner Engagement for Revising the CCMP
NBEP’s first CCMP was adopted in 1992, then updated in 2012 (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2012). In 2019, NBEP began work on this 10-year Revision. The program sought, obtained, and responded to broad input from scientists, resource managers, and environmental advocates through:

17 meetings attended by 150 individuals
two public surveys with 369 responses
30 formal presentations and hundreds of one-on-one meetings
a dedicated website providing public access to planning materials and meeting invitations

NBEP’s process focused on priority actions that NBEP and its partners have the expertise and agency to complete within ten years, rather than long lists of aspirational actions. A crosswalk of the status of 2012 CCMP Actions versus 2026 CCMP Actions highlights the more focused approach (Appendix C).
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Strategic Lenses for Action
Actions in this 2026 CCMP Revision reflect four cross-cutting issues important to the partnership:

Regionalism
Actions leverage the partnership’s unique geopolitical identity as the Narragansett Bay region to build regional partnerships for collaboration and cross-boundary coordination.
Since the publication of the 2012 CCMP, several interstate watershed groups focused on the Taunton, Blackstone, and Wood-Pawcatuck watersheds have gained strength, visibility, and agency to pursue priorities at watershed scales. The SNEP Network, which provides technical assistance services to communities throughout Southern New England, emerged in 2019 as a crucial support and networking tool for communities seeking assistance with watershed planning. NBEP and partners will build upon these strengths to continue providing services to Massachusetts and Rhode Island partners while seeking to establish new partnerships with communities and organizations in the Little Narragansett Bay watershed in Connecticut.


Connectivity
Actions reflect the importance of connection—people to people, people to natural spaces, watersheds to estuaries, and wildlife to their habitats—by protecting access, removing barriers, and facilitating dialogue.
Habitat connectivity is a prime focus area for NBEP over the next ten years. Priorities include facilitating and funding progress on reconnecting rivers and streams from their headwaters to their receiving estuaries and connecting coastal marshes to uplands to facilitate marsh migration with rising seas. Local watershed groups have gained capacity in recent years and are in good position to continue driving connectivity projects that respond specifically to needs in their watersheds. Regional salt marsh working groups, including the Salt Marsh RAMP and the Massachusetts Ecosystem Climate Adaptation Network (Mass ECAN) Salt Marsh Working Group, have collaboratively pioneered techniques for restoring and reconnecting subsiding and drowning marshes and are working together to coordinate restoration and monitoring of large projects.


Resiliency
Actions are responsive and resilient to extreme weather events, including more intense precipitation events, storm surge intensification, and higher sea levels. These impacts exacerbate coastal and rain-driven flooding while eroding coastlines, riverbanks, and marshes.
States in the Narragansett Bay region are taking lead roles in planning and funding projects to increase the region’s resilience. The Massachusetts Municipal Vulnerability Preparedness (MVP) Program, first piloted in the Taunton River watershed, was established statewide in 2017 and has since 2018 provided over $15M to communities in the Massachusetts portion of the Narragansett Bay region to increase their resiliency through vulnerability assessments and project planning. Rhode Island’s Municipal Resilience Program (MRP) has granted over $18M to cities and towns between 2020–2024 to complete municipal-driven workshop processes and implement priority projects.


Fairness
Actions strive to deliver fair distribution of benefits.
Since the publication of the 2012 CCMP, NBEP and partners have worked to plan for fairer distribution of environmental programs and benefits. For example, NBEP has intentionally invited more Massachusetts representatives to participate in its committees and working groups. As a result, NBEP has supported more projects and organizations in the region’s tributaries and watersheds in addition to projects in Narragansett Bay proper. Furthermore, NBEP has worked to engage new partners through grant programs, innovative outreach and convening, and research initiatives that elevate fishers and shellfishers, Tribes, and grassroots organizations.

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
CCMP Framework for Action
With partner consensus, NBEP set four major Goals for People, Water, Habitat, and Public Spaces. Goals are supported by four Action Plans with a hierarchy of 12 Objectives and 24 Actions for achieving those goals (Appendix B).
2026 CCMP Revision Strategy by Action Plan









PEOPLE READY TO ACT
WATER QUALITY IMPROVEMENT
HABITAT PROTECTION AND RESTORATION
PUBLIC SPACES ACCESSIBLE TO ALL




VISION: Informed and engaged partners are empowered to implement the CCMP.
VISION: Waters support healthy ecosystems and communities by meeting their designated uses, including healthy fish and wildlife habitat, drinking water supply, fishing, shellfish harvesting, and recreation.
VISION: Resilient and connected landscapes and waterways support healthy habitats and wildlife and provide people with ecosystem services like clean air and water, flood protection, recreation, and sustainable harvest.
VISION: All communities enjoy their right to safe access to nature.


GOAL: Provide foundational knowledge, capacity, and drivers for CCMP implementation.
GOAL: Improve water quality to support healthy ecosystems and communities.
GOAL: Restore and protect habitats to support wildlife and people.
GOAL: Ensure all communities realize long-term benefits of healthy watersheds.


OBJECTIVE 1: Increase meaningful and useful partner and public engagement.
OBJECTIVE 1: Improve collection and use of water quality data for decision-making.
OBJECTIVE 1: Improve collection and use of habitat data for decision-making.
OBJECTIVE 1: Increase managed public access to nature.


OBJECTIVE 2: Improve access to and use of information to improve ecosystem management and public understanding.
OBJECTIVE 2: Reduce pollution from stormwater runoff.
OBJECTIVE 2: Improve habitat resilience.
OBJECTIVE 2: Improve communication about environmental health hazards.


OBJECTIVE 3: Accelerate CCMP implementation by supporting efforts to increase regional cooperation.
OBJECTVIE 3: Reduce pollution from wastewater discharges, onsite wastewater systems, and combined sewer overflows.
OBJECTIVE 3: Improve habitat connectivity and function.




OBJECTIVE 4: Reduce trash and marine debris pollution.






Anatomy of an Action Brief

Action Description and Statement of Need – what partners and NBEP staff will do and why
Potential Projects – specific actionable tasks needed to implement the action plan
Outputs – products or services resulting from the action
Outcomes – system changes expected from completing the action
State of the Watershed Indicators – metrics by which change will be measured
Role of NBEP – specifies the service(s) that NBEP staff will provide: convene, synthesize and communicate science, fund capacity and projects
Implementing Partners – organizations that can lead or assist with action implementation due to their statutory authority, expertise, and anticipated work activities
Location – specific watershed location, if any, where the action will be directed
Timeline for Initiation – time frames for initiating the action: ongoing (initiated before 2025), short-term (1–3 years 2026–2029), medium-term (4–6 years 2030–2032), long-term (7–10 years 2033–2036)
Estimated Costs – costs for all partners over 10 years estimated at five levels (2024 dollars):

$10K–500K (NBEP staff time, capacity-building and outreach grants)
$500K–1M (convene partners, support research/modeling projects)
$1M–5M (develop and implement regional plans)
$5M–10M (project plans - designs and permits)
$10M+ (infrastructure and restoration construction)

Potential Funding Sources – organizations or grants with potential to fund projects



Supporting Documents
Implementation of the CCMP Strategy is supported by several overarching NBEP guidance documents:

The State of Narragansett Bay and Its Watershed (2017, to be updated 2027) details the status and trends for a suite of 24 stressor and condition indicators and provides insights into the ecosystem health and resilience of the Narragansett Bay region.
NBEP Research Plan (2024) created the Urban Waters Research Collaborative and guides NBEP’s scientific program, including setting targets for funding research to implement the CCMP.
NBEP Engagement Plan (to be adopted by 2029) will specify guiding principles and strategies to convene and activate partners and key audiences on priority environmental issues and promote a regional identity.
NBEP Habitat Restoration Plan (to be adopted by 2029) will outline opportunities to protect and restore key watershed and estuarine habitats.
NBEP Finance Plan (to be adopted by 2029) will describe the strategy for NBEP’s long-term financial sustainability as well as catalyze fundraising and investments for projects that implement the CCMP.

title: About the Narragansett Bay Region
text: The Narragansett Bay region spans over 2,000 square miles of land comprising the watersheds of Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, and the Coastal Salt Ponds. This landscape unites two million people across 113 cities and towns in Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Connecticut. A mosaic of urban and suburban lands is surrounded by forested landscapes and networks of rivers and streams. Reaching inland more than 50 miles, the region’s watersheds have over 4,000 miles of rivers and streams and over 40,000 acres of lakes and ponds. Salty shores stretch along 700 miles of dunes, beaches, and rocky coasts. The region hosts diverse habitats, including roughly 4,300 acres of salt marsh habitat and 875 acres of seagrass habitat, that sustain wildlife, support regional economies, and provide recreational opportunities.

title: About the Narragansett Bay Region
text:
Narragansett Bay Watershed
The Narragansett Bay watershed spans 1,705 square miles across Rhode Island and Massachusetts and includes the major tributaries of the Blackstone River, Taunton River, and Pawtuxet River that flow into the 196 square mile bay. In Narragansett Bay, salt marshes and seagrasses have declined considerably from historical extents. Fish communities are transitioning toward seasonal and mid-Atlantic species as Bay water temperatures rise. Storms have eroded Bay shorelines. Upland in the watershed, development pressure is a major stressor, causing loss of forested and wetland habitats, impacting wildlife and the health of inland and coastal waters. The watershed is fourteen percent impervious cover and half of rivers and streams are unhealthy for fish and wildlife due to excess nutrients, oxygen depletion, and other parameters (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). Thousands of barriers, including dams and improperly sized culverts, not only block migration for important diadromous fish populations, but also increase flood risk. These impacts are felt particularly in the urban areas of Providence, Worcester, Fall River, and Woonsocket, where increasingly intense rainfall events flood roads, homes, and businesses. Decades of efforts have dramatically reduced nutrient and bacteria pollution in the Bay, yet half of the estuarine waters are still unhealthy for fish and other aquatic life, with stormwater pollution driving the majority of impairments (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017).

title: About the Narragansett Bay Region
text:
Little Narragansett Bay Watershed
The Little Narragansett Bay watershed spans 317 square miles across western Rhode Island and southeastern Connecticut and includes the major tributaries of the Wood River and Pawcatuck River that flow into a 4.8 square mile estuary. Compared to the Narragansett Bay watershed, the Little Narragansett Bay watershed has one-fifth the population density and is more heavily forested and protected with conservation lands. The Barn Island Wildlife Management Area—the largest managed coastal habitat area in Connecticut—protects substantial areas of salt marsh, deciduous forest, and avian habitat. Only five percent of the watershed is impervious cover and over seventy percent of the assessed streams and rivers are fit for aquatic life (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). Increasingly, the major watershed stressor is development-driven changes in land use. Water quality impacts tend to be concentrated near these expanding developed areas. Within the bay proper, seagrasses remain scarce and shellfishing is prohibited due to water quality concerns.

title: About the Narragansett Bay Region
text:
Coastal Salt Ponds Watersheds
The Coastal Salt Ponds watersheds span 56 square miles along the southern coast of Rhode Island, running from Point Judith in the east to Little Narragansett Bay in the west. The Rhode Island Coastal Salt Ponds—Judith Pond, Potter’s Pond, Cards Pond, Trustom Pond, Greenhill Pond, Ninigret Pond, Quonochontaug Pond, Winnapaug Pond, and Maschaug Pond—function as coastal estuarine lagoons connected to the ocean by inlets or breachways through barrier beaches. The ponds are shallow and brackish, which make them valuable as fish and shellfish nurseries but also susceptible to eutrophication and bacterial loading. Each pond has a unique identity and supports iconic wildlife and coastal habitats as well as many important commercial and recreational activities, including aquaculture. Pond assessments show 85% are fit for aquatic wildlife and 100% are fit for recreation (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). The long stretches of adjacent sandy beaches draw locals and visitors alike, making them important recreational and economic resources. Population size and development have grown across the salt ponds region in the last twenty years, though the population density is still only half that of the Narragansett Bay watershed. Rising sea levels and altered hydrology are major stressors for these unique coastal salt pond habitats causing erosion and subsidence that outpace marsh accretion.

title: Action Habitat-2.1: Habitat Plans
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Update existing and develop new regional habitat restoration plans and strategies to facilitate cross-boundary projects and address emerging challenges for habitat resilience.

title: Action Habitat-2.1: Habitat Plans
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will update existing and publish new regional plans for freshwater and estuarine habitat restoration. ‘Plans’ refers broadly to strategies, designs, assessments, and protocols that apply from an ecosystem to a habitat location. Restoration plans have been developed for salt marshes, shellfish, coastal ponds, reefs, rivers, and forests. Seagrass and oyster restoration planning, along with broader aquaculture oversight, are underway locally and in the greater New England region.
Many existing plans were developed more than a decade ago and need to be updated to reflect a regional approach to emerging challenges for habitat resilience. Some are confined by geopolitical boundaries and may lack specific implementation details necessary to ensure responsive projects. Opportunities exist to improve cross-state and other regional coordination, collaboration, and information sharing about habitat restoration, land use planning, and biodiversity conservation (Stevens et al. 2023).
Involvement of watershed-based organizations, land trusts, commercial aquaculture organizations, and other experts working at the local scale is also important for establishing watershed and estuarine habitat restoration priorities. Local and regional collaboration ensures that priorities can be guided by partners most familiar with local issues and supported by partners who are experienced with working at larger scales.
NBEP will convene partners to share restoration expertise and emerging best practices, coordinate habitat planning efforts, and prioritize restoration projects across institutional and state boundaries, with a goal of developing the NBEP Habitat Restoration Plan by 2028. NBEP will also assist to leverage resources with projects like the Blackstone Needs Assessment (2019–2021), the Blackstone Fish Passage Project (2021–current), and the Salt Marsh Restoration, Assessment and Monitoring Program (RAMP) (2022–current). NBEP will help fund partner and community outreach, efforts to update planning documents, and research to fill knowledge gaps.

title: Action Habitat-2.1: Habitat Plans
text:
Potential Projects

Develop the NBEP Habitat Restoration Plan.
Update Rhode Island Anadromous Fish Restoration Plan.
Update existing restoration strategies to include updated data, information, and completed projects.
Develop a Narragansett Bay region salt marsh restoration strategy or update the Rhode Island Coastal Wetland Restoration Strategy to include the upper part of Narragansett Bay in Massachusetts and portions of Little Narragansett Bay in Connecticut, where useful. Strategy should highlight areas where marsh migration is possible and should be supported.
Support the development and implementation of the Rhode Island Shellfish Restoration and Enhancement Plan.
Develop a Narragansett Bay and/or broader regional submerged aquatic vegetation restoration plan that reviews restoration strategies that have been tried in Narragansett Bay to date, including current seed-based restoration strategies, and assesses whether recent water quality improvements could improve restoration success. This restoration plan should also include new data collected on flowering phenology and seed collection and seed transfer guidance.
Expand and update Sea Level Affecting Marsh Migration (SLAMM) models to include the Palmer and Runnins Rivers and other areas of MA, CT, and RI that were not covered in state models.
Pursue or expand state level management plans for lakes and ponds, inclusive of invasive species and educational outreach to boaters.
Develop plans and actions to protect cold-water streams and rivers, or specific endangered and/or threatened freshwater species, such as native brook trout.

title: Action Habitat-2.3: Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Assess and restore benthic habitats to support dynamic estuarine ecosystems.
crumbs: Action Habitat-2.3: Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration

title: Action Habitat-2.3: Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will assess and accelerate restoration activities for key estuarine benthic habitats. The seagrasses, hard-bottom habitats, and other benthos that exist on the bottom of the estuarine waters of Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, and the Coastal Salt Ponds support diverse ecosystems, aquaculture, fisheries, and recreation. Understanding how these habitats have changed under shifting conditions, including improved water quality conditions, warmer water temperatures, and higher water levels, is key to advancing restoration activities.
Sediment Profile Images (SPI) throughout Narragansett Bay show improvements in benthic habitats between 2008 and 2018 due to the reduction of nutrients loading from WWTFs (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2020). Sled cameras have also shown changes in the organisms living on top of the sediments in the upper portions of the Bay. Partners should consider prioritizing follow-up benthic assessments during the 10-year life of this CCMP Revision.
Two types of seagrasses exist in the Narragansett Bay region: eelgrass (Zostera marina) and widgeon grass (Ruppia maritima). Water pollution, a wasting disease outbreak in the 1930s, the Hurricane of 1938, and coastal development caused severe damage to seagrass beds. Where once eelgrass was found as far north as Providence, eelgrass currently thrives only in the lower bay, the Coastal Salt Ponds, and the outer parts of Little Narragansett Bay (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2023). Eelgrass extent has increased with the improvement of water quality, especially in the West Passage. However, eelgrass extent has declined in the Coastal Ponds and shallow embayments likely due to high water temperatures stressing the plants (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2023). Also of note is a persistent bed of widgeon grass in Greenwich Bay which provides some hope that eelgrass may return to the upper bay (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2023).
Artificial reefs have long been used as restoration techniques to improve the attraction and production of key marine species, including lobsters, shellfish, sea bass and other sport fish, thus improving recreational and economic opportunities. In 2019, RIDEM and TNC-RI installed a series of “reef balls”, artificial reef structures in the Providence River near East Providence to enhance fish communities. Since then, RIDEM and TNC-RI have continued monitoring the fish communities in the area. URI is researching the possibility of adding artificial reefs off the south shore of Rhode Island to protect the region from storms and to enhance habitat including lobster rearing and kelp production.
Renewed interest in these habitats, including federal funding to support seagrass research, continued SPI analysis (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2020), the formation of the Seaweed Ecology and Assessment Collaborative, and continued fish community monitoring highlights the need to understand the variety of stressors that impact benthic habitats. NBEP can synthesize what has been done to assess and restore these habitats and analyze the data collected to inform policy decisions and future management actions, including restoration.
NBEP will continue to convene and coordinate partners across state and institutional boundaries to coordinate and share knowledge, practices, and outcomes of assessing and restoring benthic habitats.
crumbs: Action Habitat-2.3: Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration

title: Action Habitat-2.3: Benthic Habitat Assessment and Restoration
text:
Potential Projects

Synthesize past and ongoing regional seagrass restoration efforts including methodologies used, success of restoration efforts, locations, etc. This synthesis should assess if these techniques could be more successful with current, improved water quality.
Update seagrass site suitability models or indices (Detenbeck and Rego 2015). Ensure such models include environmental variables of interest including water quality, sediment type and quality. Focus should be given to shallow water habitats to ensure data coverage is of sufficient resolution for a model.
Update monitoring protocols to modernize approaches to tiered seagrass monitoring, include widgeon grass, and develop sentinel seagrass monitoring sites. Sentinel sites should focus on how habitats respond to stress, establishing baselines for restoration, and include an area currently covered with widgeon grass to assess any potential transition from widgeon grass to eelgrass.
Establish long-term goals for benthic habitat monitoring, including the use of sediment profile imaging, sled-cameras, gliders, and autonomous underwater vehicles.
Update 2018 benthic sediment profile imaging (SPI) for Narragansett Bay. Expand SPI to other areas of the region (Salt Ponds and Little Narragansett Bay) and new sites of interest, including shallow water portions of the coastal ponds. SPI can provide an assessment of benthic habitat quality trends, particularly in the upper part of the Bay due to improved water quality.
Update benthic sled camera monitoring in the Providence River Estuary.
Synthesize artificial reef work that has been completed throughout the bay assessing the method and materials used, and if the reef has improved fish habitat in the area. Use this synthesis to develop an artificial reef plan to guide future work to focus on both habitat creation and resilience in high-erosion areas.
Implement the Macroalgae Monitoring Protocol developed by SEAC-RI to understand the seasonal abundance/distribution of drift and attached algae, habitat impacts from drift algae, and changes to algae due to stress.
Explore using dredged materials to restore areas of exposed sediment containing high concentrations of contaminants.

title: Action Habitat-3.2: Salt Marsh Restoration
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Restore coastal wetlands and reconnect with uplands for habitat migration, wildlife, and recreation.

title: Action Habitat-3.2: Salt Marsh Restoration
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will accelerate salt marsh restoration using techniques to restore marsh hydrology and upland connectivity and ensure resilience of these habitats and their associated wildlife to sea level rise. Sustaining salt marshes involves a variety of techniques to restore and enhance subsiding marshes and to conserve adjacent uplands for marshes to migrate inland as sea levels rise (McKown et al. 2024).
The natural hydrology of tidal marshes has been impacted by agriculture, mosquito ditching, and other infrastructure that prevents water from draining from the marsh after rains and high tides. Impounded water causes native marsh plants adapted to drier conditions at low tide to die, resulting in subsidence of the marsh surface (Pappal and Kahl 2022). Locally-pioneered restoration techniques involve digging shallow creeks or runnels to facilitate gradual water drainage and placing sediment to elevate degraded marshes to make them more resilient to sea level rise (Save The Bay 2021). These techniques have been used in several Rhode Island locations, including Succotash Marsh in South Kingstown where NBEP is funding the planning and design phases of a project managed by the Narragansett Bay National Estuarine Research Reserve.
Accelerating sea level rise is outpacing natural salt marsh accretion, causing marshes to erode and drown in place (Kutcher et al., n.d.). Marshes can migrate inland onto low-lying areas, but this process may be impeded by built structures, invasive species, or steep topography, among other barriers (Burman et al. 2023). MA DER is funding tidal restriction prioritization and field assessment work in the Massachusetts portion of the Narragansett Bay region to identify priority barriers to marsh migration and aquatic connectivity. Partners must identify opportunities to conserve adjacent uplands in densely populated and urbanized areas for future inland marsh migration. Because most properties adjacent to salt marshes are privately owned, managed migration will involve a variety of land use changes, including land acquisition, redevelopment planning, rolling easements, and setback and buffer definitions. Proactive community engagement is therefore a crucial aspect of salt marsh resilience planning and implementation.
NBEP will continue to convene and coordinate partners across state and institutional boundaries to coordinate and share knowledge, practices, and outcomes of saltmarsh restoration projects in the Salt Marsh RAMP workgroup. NBEP will fund capacity for community outreach, planning, and implementation, and support partners by seeking external funding.

Facilitate marsh migration through land conservation and managed retreat through land acquisition, redevelopment planning, rolling easements, setbacks, and buffer definitions.
Deliver ongoing one-on-one technical assistance to municipal parks and land use programs.
Identify tidal restriction restoration opportunities in concert with other restoration techniques.
Maintain the Salt Marsh RAMP workgroup project tool, MarshMap, and expand the tool to include Massachusetts and Connecticut projects.

title: Action People-1.1: Engagement Plan
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Develop and implement the NBEP Engagement Plan to convene and activate partners to implement priority CCMP actions.

title: Action People-1.1: Engagement Plan
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will develop a coordinated and collaborative NBEP Engagement Plan to guide NBEP’s convening services and partnership-building activities. The Engagement Plan will include work to articulate and promote a unique identity for the Narragansett Bay region that highlights specific CCMP implementation opportunities and challenges within the broader southern New England context (see Finance Plan Action: People-3.1).
Successful implementation of the NBEP CCMP requires an informed, engaged, and empowered community of partners making choices and taking actions that increase environmental health and resilience. Coproduction of knowledge and management solutions can build trust among resource users and managers and provide deeper understandings and diverse perspectives about what the people of the Narragansett Bay region value most about the environment. NBEP seeks to energize effective partnerships to implement CCMP actions and develop a strategic framework of networks and services to support partner needs for targeted engagement throughout the watershed.
NBEP will lead the development and regular update of the Engagement Plan to describe, prioritize, coordinate, and drive NBEP’s convening, outreach, education, and communication activities necessary to implement the CCMP and promote a unique regional identity. The Engagement Plan will guide meaningful collaboration with people and communities most impacted by pressing environmental issues, such as nonprofits, tribes, municipalities, academic researchers, community leaders, businesses, and more. It will also facilitate delivery of responsive, research-based outreach programs focused on priority topics.
The Engagement Plan will coordinate and amplify ongoing efforts and identify opportunities and gaps that can be met by existing or new programs and funding mechanisms. It will also broaden engagement approaches historically limited by organizational or political boundaries to address issues at environmentally meaningful watershed scales. The Engagement Plan will prioritize, focus, and empower engagement efforts to optimize collaboration and support for implementing CCMP objectives and actions. This coordinated approach will inspire new collaborations among existing programs, reinforce existing partnerships, and optimize messaging effectiveness.
Implementing the Engagement Plan requires NBEP to continue hosting quarterly Executive and Steering Committee meetings, biannual Science Advisory Committee meetings, and subject-specific subcommittees and working groups as needed. NBEP will also host topic-specific events, including the Annual Science Event, and offer convening services for partner-led workshops, committees, or meetings to implement partner strategic plans, workplans, or equivalent planning documents that align with the CCMP and the Engagement Plan. NBEP will regularly update the Engagement Plan to adapt to changing conditions in the environment and needs of the people in the region.

title: Action People-2.1: Science Communication Tools
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Develop compelling open-source data visualizations, stories, and dashboards.

title: Action People-2.1: Science Communication Tools
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will develop science communication to inform and engage partners and other interested people on estuary and watershed science and restoration.
Synthesizing and communicating estuarine and watershed science is foundational to protecting and restoring water quality and the ecological integrity of the Narragansett Bay region. Too often, science-based information is presented in dense academic literature and government reports, uses language that is not fully understood by non-scientists, and is in a form that is not available or useful to end users with the skills and agency to act. Additionally, information that is freely available may be out of date or incorrect and some information may only be accessible to a small number of people within an organization.
NBEP strives to develop accurate, understandable, accessible, and up-to-date communication tools for groups as diverse as scientists, managers, public officials, educators, and private citizens. NBEP also hosts current and past plans, documents, and datasets in its website’s Resource Library and GIS Data Hub. Using data visualizations and dashboards to display environmental status and trends is a simple and elegant way to accurately and broadly communicate information in a timely, accessible fashion. Narrative stories and StoryMaps are also effective tools. Creating these tools using open-source software that is freely available without a commercial license ensures that tools can be widely shared, modified, and accessible to all.
Through its roles to convene and synthesize and communicate science, NBEP will facilitate widespread adoption of open-source tools; collaborate with data developers, partners, and end users to develop tools that respond to current needs; and advertise new tools and develop training sessions for the tools, if necessary.

title: Action People-2.3: Indicators
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Develop and update indicators to track environmental and socioeconomic trends.

title: Action People-2.3: Indicators
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action calls NBEP and partners to develop and update indicators to track environmental and socioeconomic trends. Stressor indicators provide information about water quality, landscape and chemical processes that can cause changes in the estuaries and their watersheds. Condition indicators provide information about the status and trends of natural ecosystems and human socioeconomic systems, like public health (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017).
NBEP convened more than 50 experts from universities, agencies, and organizations in Rhode Island and Massachusetts to develop environmental indicators and evaluate their status and trends. The resulting report identified and analyzed the status and trends of 24 key environmental and socioeconomic indicators (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). The geographic scope of most indicators was limited to Narragansett Bay and its watershed. Since the report’s publication, some indicators have been updated and expanded to include the Coastal Salt Ponds and Little Narragansett Bay. In addition, NBEP has refined three indicators to include new information, including Nuisance Flooding (Sea Level), Atmospheric Nitrogen Deposition (Nutrient Loading), and Solar Project Installations in Forests (Land Use) (Appendix A). NBEP continues to work with the Southeast New England Program to align indicator development to improve efficiency and create consistent messaging.
In 2023, NBEP’s Social Science Working Group nominated four new socioeconomic indicators for development, including Public Access, Resilient Municipalities, Public Health, and Economy. NBEP will develop and implement Public Access and Resilient Municipalities indicators over the next ten years (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program et al. 2023).
NBEP will lead development, metric refinement, and updates of environmental and socioeconomic indicators as guided by the Research Plan, Science Advisory Committee, Steering Committee, and topical work groups. NBEP will also expand the geographic scope of indicators to include Little Narragansett Bay and Rhode Island Coastal Ponds watersheds. Results will be published as an update to The State of Narragansett Bay and Its Watershed report.

title: Action People-3.2: Regional Targets
text:
NoteFull Action Statement



Establish shared regional ecosystem targets with public reporting systems to track progress.

title: Action People-3.2: Regional Targets
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action calls for NBEP to lead the development of priority regional ecosystem targets for the Narragansett Bay region to motivate collective action.
Effective protection and restoration of water, habitats, fish and wildlife, and quality of life in the Narragansett Bay region depends on 1) understanding the status and trends of key environmental and socioeconomic indicators; 2) establishing measurable targets for those indicators that equate to a desired future condition; and 3) adopting reporting systems to monitor progress and foster additional collaborative work to reach targets.
In 2017, NBEP and partners identified and characterized the status and trends of 24 key stressor and condition indicators underlying the ecosystem health of Narragansett Bay and its communities (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2017). NBEP and partners will continue to develop indicators (Action: People-2.3) and monitor and model their status and trends (Actions: Water-1.1, Habitat-1.1, and People-2.2).
NBEP will collaborate with partners to develop targets at appropriate scales that define desired future conditions for indicators. Target development will take into consideration existing targets, such as those required by the Clean Water Act to support designated uses of waters, Total Maximum Daily Loads (TMDLs), Special Areas Management Plans (SAMPs), and habitat restoration plans. Targets should encompass topics that NBEP and partners have agency and capacity to advance and ideally derive from metrics that NBEP already measures for State of the Watershed indicators (e.g., habitat acres, pollution reductions, number of public access points).

title: Action Public Spaces-1.1: New Public Access
text: Assess, prioritize, and support plans and projects to expand public access to waterways, natural areas, and urban green spaces.
crumbs: Action Public Spaces-1.1: New Public Access

title: Action Public Spaces-1.1: New Public Access
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will develop science to inform the creation of new public access to waterways, natural areas, and urban green spaces across the region. This information can help inform the design of access points that are resilient to storms, responsive to potential ecological impacts from overuse (Narragansett Bay Estuary Program 2021), and deliver more access through community-centered siting and design.
There are hundreds of natural parks, thousands of urban green spaces, and hundreds of miles of bikeways open to the public; there are hundreds of saltwater access points and thousands for freshwater throughout the region. While most residents in the Narragansett Bay region live within a mile of a waterway and open green space, easy access is still a challenge for some, creating the need to strategically prioritize new and upgraded public access points to these natural amenities.
Hundreds of years of development, industry, and shifting policies have limited access to water and natural lands, sometimes by physical obstruction, sometimes due to polluted waters, sometimes by legal right. Areas with working waterfronts, shoreline armoring, and privatization restrict certain types of use. Rivers and streams have been dammed and completely paved over, hiding and limiting their amenities. Designating new access points or restoring historical access is a challenge as many agencies and departments within state and local governments and often private landowners may be involved (Smith 2023).
Growth and shifts in population centers and recreational preferences also leave gaps in fair access. Travel time, transportation, parking availability, mobility, and non-accessible programming can pose barriers to accessing nature. An individual’s financial resources and the quality of the natural amenities near their home may also impact their access. There are measurable regional disparities in access, with race and ethnicity strongly correlated to travel distance to high quality public coastal amenities (Twichell et al. 2022).
NBEP will develop a new Public Access Indicator and publish a public access database with waterway and green space access sites, fund capacity for project planning for new public access, and support non-governmental organizations, local people, and local and state governments to accelerate projects in areas that lack quality access to nature.

title: Action Public Spaces-1.3: Public Access Managemen

title: Action Public Spaces-1.3: Public Access Managemen
text: Support long-term management and stewardship of public access points through community engagement, policy change, and job creation.
crumbs: Action Public Spaces-1.3: Public Access Managemen

title: Action Public Spaces-1.3: Public Access Managemen
section: Action Description and Statement of Need
text:
Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will increase capacity for sustainable management of public access facilities, while improving user experience and accessibility for public enjoyment of the region’s natural areas. Target solutions include enhanced volunteer engagement, innovative funding solutions, and workforce development.
Inequities exist across the region in ease of access to high quality nature experiences (Twichell et al. 2022). Site quality factors such as safety, trash, and perceived water quality conditions impact visitor preferences. Beyond routine maintenance, many access points need upgraded infrastructure and amenities to equitably serve visitors and protect the environment. Such upgrades may include parking areas (especially for waterway access points), multilingual signage, Americans with Disabilities (ADA) trails and boat facilities, trash and recycling bins, and restrooms.
A variety of federal, state, municipal, and non-governmental organizations own and manage various public access facilities such as trails, boat launches, campgrounds, beaches, playgrounds, and urban parks. Taxes, entry fees, non-profit memberships, and donations are typical income sources used for maintenance. However, competing needs for limited funds can result in deferred maintenance (National Park Service 2022), resulting in reduced visitor enjoyment that leads to reduced visitation and revenue. One-time capital grants for park improvements often do not include funding for long-term maintenance. Innovative funding strategies could be pursued to create sustainable funding streams for operation and maintenance to stay ahead of deferred maintenance (Cohen 2015).
Volunteers often provide in-kind stewardship services and develop a sense of ownership of a particular place. To help build further capacity for community-based management support, non-governmental organizations have created workforce training programs like Groundwork Southcoast’s Green Team and the Woonasquatucket River Watershed Council’s River Rangers to maintain and support ongoing projects and natural spaces.
NBEP will fund capacity to develop robust volunteer recruitment and training, as well as workforce training, for parks and recreation management. NBEP will voice support for development of new policies that support sustainable funding for maintenance and operations.
